material from my own projects so it diddnt do much by this time i had spent about months on programming i then took about a month break from programming to finish college harder classes i then started again after the break month started javascript again and built this node js based bot using ibm watson conversation and botkit ai that integrated with slack facebook it was based on a local store and allowed you to place orders via chat no backend integration just a sample chatbot month this is the first time i built something big i created this web app with a nodejs express backend postgresql database sequelize orm and it served up handlebars bootstrap pages it allows people to schedule and manage home services such as lawn mowing junk removal etc i also built a contractor portal to keep track of all the jobs posted map them with google maps api keep track of paid jobs send sms alerts etc this took probably weeks working hours a day days a week but i learned a lot about server side programming done i just need to integrate a payment system like square api or something month i found a react native hello world tutorial on youtube and got my hello world app working testable on my phone i then began adding feature at a time getting most of my information from the react native docs and expo docs which are both great i spent about hours a day days a week for weeks learning different features ways to structure apps and adding to my app as i learned it eventually turned into mintnote if anyone wants to check out the app its free and leave some ratings or submit feedback i would really appreciate being able to see what you think there is a feedback option in the app also landing page text yourself a link ios app store google play store throughout this process i kept a detailed notebook using microsoft onenote taking detailed notes on everything i did my main philosophy was to spend the minimum amount of time as possible learning reading about a new technology usually hours i get into actually building something where i can see results as fast as possible i am not recommending everyone learning programming follow what i did i wanted to be able to build fast mvp products for web apps mobile apps and that s what i can do now depending on what your goals are you should tailor your learning strategy to achieve them', 'here s a long rant because i ve been meaning to open up about the real world of the programming job hunt for a while and this is the first forum i m letting my wrath out on let me rephrase your question someone comes you and says they really want to get a job as an electrician they ve been looking at wire cutters soldering irons and reading about voltage they wonder whether it would be better to start learning how to use the wire cutters or if they should just buy insulated boots and start stomping around in them you see what i mean if you want to learn to code there are paths you can go down this depends on what kind of person you are and what you really want path or just learn programming first do you like logic as in socrates is a man all men are mortal therefore socrates is mortal this is like going to college for that ee degree find a free tutorial on javascript and learn how to do basic things like make a guess the number game in text expand from there you will pick up other avenues to go down as you play around all you need to get started in javascript is right click in your browser inspect go to the console and start typing if you want to get real fancy use something like sublime free version or notepad and it ll help point out when you screw up while you re typing free and easy to start javascript also plugs neatly into html css and thanks to all sorts of frameworks it does basically anything nowadays i made a virtual reality game for smartphone in a javascript framework don t let anybody talk down to you about starting in humble javascript it is now essentially possible to code a whole enterprise system on nothing but javascript even if your tools convert it into other languages behind the scenes using path you can code all sorts of games and there s a zillion tutorials online start with simple text games like guess a number then use maybe a framework like jquery to manipulate things on the web page and you can quickly scale up to pac man using nothing but free front end tools heck now with html css you can do crazy things without ever hitting the real programming languages you could do a whole choose your own adventure with html css without even touching javascript what s a choose your own adventure except some links between pages random js link path or just make a game already are you serious about the game thing honestly there s a huuuuge gap between how coding is taught and how programmers work in the real world just like electricians if a kid goes to college in ee day one is all about the syllabus and maybe voltage and ohm s law in the real world if a kid walked up to you and wanted to start work today i bet the first thing you d teach the kid is how to stay insulated keep one hand in your pocket around mains that sort of thing assuming you even work around mains power maybe you work around a zillion amps and lesson one is stay ten feet back until you know what you re doing maybe you do volt computer circuits and you don t have to worry around anything other than bigass capacitors those bits while they need to be backed up by theory are way more important and necessary than theory when you re doing actual work like most real programmers if they re not on linux server command line level aren t going to let you touch a line of code until you either open a git repository or clone one off github maybe they use subversion same idea the differences only widen from there true head down programming isn t really that big a part of most people s lives unless they re deep into a project during crunch time it s a lot more common than anyone likes to end up spending two days bouncing around meetings because the client wants to do something stupid and you really really really want them to change their course of action before anyone codes a single line if you can work with a professional maybe you ll find out the part you really like is d modeling and programming only needs to happen to make the models move around who knows if you can t find a professional to crib off of and please check your network to see if you can then skip the tools bit and pick a classic but simplish game you like and want to copy there are a zillion tutorials for instance on how to create a basic first person shooter in the free to use unity and unreal engines you ll only have to learn programming when you want to code custom behaviors like ai or whatever other fun stuff you wanna add because game programmers don t actually make games art departments directors etc do game programmers just have to do things like make the ai take cover better because they re too easy to kill or to solve why the game counts your bullets wrong when you reload after shooting half a clip nobody really wants to be a game programmer they want to be a game maker and programming is one tool along the way you could make a very basic start on a doom or halo clone today in unreal tutorials to clone final fantasy in rpgmaker are a dime a dozen humble bundle has pretty regular sales where you can get a whole development environment and books and tutorials for like there s currently two bundles of books to get you started at starting at wanna be like vlambeer and code a mobile friendly game in gamemaker humble bundle has pretty regular sales where you can get a lifetime subscription for like', 'hi guys over the past years of programming professional i ve distilled a lot of my experience into logical steps to learn a new language quickly and thoroughly i hope you get something useful out of this if you have any questions let me know below here are ways you get shortcut your learning process use knowledge that you know from other languages nail down the fundamental knowledge resources that will help you learn fast find what you don t understand and work backwards try first then ask use knowledge that you know from other languages the fastest way to learn a new language is to already know at least one use what you know from a previous language to learn a new one for that i use what i love about hyperpolyglot is the table format comparing multiple languages it s easy to see relationships between what you already know and how you d implement them in the new language want to see how you d do variable interpolation in node js but you only know python no problem have a look at the reference sheet under variable interpolation sometimes what you know in one language doesn t translate to to the new language that s okay just learn the differences for instance in php you have class property visibility such as private but in python there isn t an exact translation private class properties in python are denoted by an underscore or double underscore which mangles the instance variables name the manipulation of them are more left to convention if a python developer sees an underscore they know that by convention they should avoid manipulating it directly but nothing really stops them from doing so added although transliterating one language to another is definitely an easy and fast route each language has their own idioms which will force you to think in new ways idioms will also make it easy for other developers to quickly understand the code you wrote that being said if you learn something in one language there s nothing wrong with implementing some flavor of it in the new language if it s still concise and clearly understandable for instance javascript wasn t intended to be a functional programming language but it has language constructs such as functions as first class citizens that allows for it someone thought they d try programming in the functional paradigm in javascript it turned out just fine this isn t always true but do what s best for the project we could go one step further and learn the differences between the languages for instance learning channels in go for concurrency is pretty unique to the actual language but the general concept of concurrency and shared nothing is pretty universal nail down the fundamental knowledge i think a lot of learning problems come from jumping into the deep end too early they rush into coding say for instance from a tutorial without truly understanding the fundamentals do not skimp on the fundamentals yes it can be a bit difficult to understand yes it s not as fun as going straight into coding but it will shortcut and accelerate your learning it will reduce your frustrations when something doesn t work you ll start to realize and understand why now please don t take it as me saying don t learn by coding that s not what i m saying i m saying don t jump into coding while winging the fundamentals don t jump into learning rails while ignoring learning ruby don t jump into vue js without learning javascript don t jump into postgres without learning data modeling and indexes don t jump into writing an http api without learning http tcp ip caching rpc and rest understand variable scope before you use them understand closures and its relationship to variable scope before you use them understand generators before you write your own understand promises and its association to asynchronous programming if you skip these fundamentals in a language that has them you ll see yourself often confused resources that will help you learn fast these resources will help you learn the basics of a language quickly awesome everything a few years ago someone started curated links around certain languages and frameworks and posting them as repos on github i m not sure who it was but i am grateful to that person if you ve ever seen the icon on the left you ve undoubtedly experienced an awesome list there s pretty much a curated list for everything css react go design patterns scaling big data machine learning and the list goes on if you want to learn say python just go to google and type in awesome python try it now with whatever you re trying to learn awesome programming language sometimes there will be multi repos with awesome lists on the same topic but perhaps with separate links pick and choose resources from those lists that help you learn fundamentals about that language find what you don t understand and work backwards having a particular difficulty first realize what you don t understand and that scope of it work backwards by diving deep into the fundamental concepts around the problem that you re having for instance if you are writing an anonymous function in a loop you re trying to use a variable that s in the same scope in the anonymous function but you see that it only ever uses the last value learn what closures are and how they work you ll learn that the anonymous function is actually a closure not in all languages and apart from having access to variables inside of the closure it also captures a reference to the variables outside of the closure learn more about closures in javascript read as much as you need to to fully understand the problem you re going through i find that reading as much code that i can mostly open source repos on github in the target language i want to learn uncovers pretty much most things you need to know about the language find what you don t understand and work backwards as described above try first then ask when trying to learn a programming language and anything else such as a framework or a paradigm it is my opinion that you should try to make an effort to learn on your own first there are plenty of resources available that you could search on google that there s no excuse in this age of information to not at least be able to find information first read it and try yourself now after trying on your own to learn you get stuck and not able to find information online that helps you get unstuck or you need fundamental knowledge that isn t anywhere then go ahead and ask experienced people questions this shows that you ve done your homework and that you value their time there are many places to find these experts slack communities in your particular region or ones around a particular topic the trusty irc channels reddit forums gitter and sometimes even twitter keeping all of the above points will help supercharge your learning not only in your beginning stages of learning an additional language but throughout your career if you liked this article please share it with those that you think would benefit from it as i ve mentioned if you have any questions around the learning process of learning a new language please let me know below']

Name: 15_linguistics_nlp_computational linguistics_natural
Representative_Docs: ['i study both natural language processing nlp computational linguistics and programming language theory plt albeit i study them separately from one another it seems like you re asking whether there are people who apply linguistic analysis to programming languages in the same way that we analyze natural languages the short answer is no not in the same way phonology and morphology do not exist in programming languages and although programming languages have syntax and semantics they are not studied through the same lens as natural languages plt does investigate syntax and semantics of programming languages however since programming languages are completely artificial studying a language s syntax is as simple as looking up the formal specification there s no scientific observation of how people naturally use the language since everything is formal and mathematical it s extremely different from natural language syntax analysis there are some parallels to be sure but not as many as you may think semantics of programming languages are also formally specified again this is in contrast to natural languages where there are no formal specifications essentially there isn t really a way to analyze programming languages the same way as natural languages the overlap of linguistics with cs is called computational linguistics cl cl is closely connected to nlp though nlp is specifically concerned with giving computers the ability to comprehend human speech text these are fields which are currently expanding a lot so if you re interested in natural languages and computer science then these might be a good place to look into', 'one of my favorite classes in college was a computational linguistics course i had an excellent professor with a background in linguistics and phd in computational linguistics that made the course fair but challenging i would suggest a possible route for you as completing your degree gt building up good foundations in computer science and programming gt apply for a graduate program in computational linguistics or computer science gt continue building up knowledge of ml and linguistics gt profit there s a tremendous amount of math and stats involved in computational linguistics and there s a lot of machine learning particularly on the side of nlp i m still a novice in the linguistics side but i think building up a good cs background is equally if not more important good luck', 'i ve done some stuff in nlp but not in computational linguistics they are related but not quite the same i would say that nlp is more technical focused i would say than linguistics even if we re talking about computational linguistics computational linguistics you ll be doing something more like creating parse trees while in nlp you ll be exploring algorithms to determine how statistically likely one word is to come after another based on trained language model or working on speech recognition now there is linguistics without the computational part which is kind of related but not really computer science based as an example here are the descriptions of the computational linguistics course and the natural language computing course at my university computational linguistics computational linguistics and the processing of language by computer topics include context free grammars chart parsing statistical parsing semantics and semantic interpretation ambiguity resolution techniques reference resolution emphasis on statistical learning methods for lexical syntactic and semantic knowledge natural language processing introduction to techniques involving natural language and speech in applications such as information retrieval extraction and filtering intelligent web searching spelling and grammar checking speech recognition and synthesis and multi lingual systems including machine translation n grams pos tagging semantic distance metrics indexing on line lexicons and thesauri markup languages collections of on line documents corpus analysis perl and other software']

Name: 16_degree_cs_cs degree_education
Representative_Docs: ['not me but i have a close friend who is way more successful than me in cs and he doesn t even have a bachelor degree yet he s running an advanced tech startup worth millions in silicon valley i ve watched him hatch his career from the very beginning and i ve learned a lot based on what he s done and how he did it the first thing to do without a degree is to simply get noticed have a portfolio of well designed projects that showcase expertise in your chosen field and position them up front and center on your github profile better yet start a medium dev blog and communicate your process and methodology have a twitter linkedin and interact with well known professionals and put your opinions on current happenings in your field and genuinely engage with others doing the same on the other side of the coin let s say you re not a self starter whiz kid that can do all that just yet there is still hope to getting professional status take advantage of moocs and ideally ones that offer certifications you may have to pay several hundred dollars and the certificate might not mean much but what is valuable is the knowledge and the community of fellow alumni you re now in within this community you can do what i stated above which is showcase your learning process in dev blogs continually update your portfolio to reflect your knowledge breadth and depth and engage with others trying to do the same thing unless you re publishing seminal pieces of research or writing viral blog posts you re only gonna get attention by giving others it first additionally some learning platforms like udacity have hiring partners or job specific postings to help their alumni get placed in person boot camps also have high job placement rates but also cost the equivalent of a semester at a state college with your online presence established you can provided a resume that stands out to potential employers when looking for junior entry level positions there are millions of new cs grads every year all of whom have completed a bs ms you can t possibly expect to compete unless you have an actual presence in your field so when the recruiter is deciding between average joe schmo with a gpa that has a bs in cs from osu and you the guy running a popular blog called down to dl with posts like explorations of lstms in deep learning who is the more interesting candidate the simple fact that you won t be able to complete many online applications without saying you have a degree further drives this point gt do you have any advice to pass on to others in your situation were you scared when i found out my friend wasn t going to go the traditional college route i was scared for him but surprisingly he wasn t it s not like he was a gpa super genius in highschool that was too good for college he just simply enjoyed his niche in cs too much to fail that drove home the point to me that legitimate passion for what you re doing will carry you a lot further than your name printed on a degree it has to be your obsession that being said you can always get some simple certs for it networking sysadmin and there is almost always a job available in that field however for most other dedicated programming fields you ll be best served using the aforementioned advice instead', 'gt how many recruiters would bother to look at my portfolio and how can i make sure i stand out from the other cs degrees how can i make up for lack of cs degree prove you can build stuff by actually building stuff not tutorial projects but hours of real work building something that serves a real purpose even if you never get a lot of users for example lichess com although that has a huge number of users but it s literally just a side project to scratch an itch describe what you built using meaningful details that show how you can contribute to a team building stuff for a business this also depends on what type of software you re doing webdev is the low hanging fruit as far as entry without a degree machine learning seems to be about the polar opposite no degree don t waste your time other fields probably fall somewhere in the middle gt even if i get a entry job would my lack of cs degree harm my overall software engineer career or would i be judged by my merit alone again depends on the job i do webdev a cs degree is overkill for what i do definitely not useless but also definitely not necessary you can learn all the basics in about a year six months if you push really really hard and are super focused on the essentials on the other hand if you want to do machine learning then you better get a masters or phd gt what s with the mismatch between the statistical data and how hard time people are having getting into entry jobs squeaky wheel bias people having no trouble don t post about how they have no trouble my last job search took me one month to find a new remote job with only year experience and no cs degree under my belt granted i m not getting paid k but neither are most developers if you re only looking for k or you re only looking in one area or you just suck at applying seriously it s a skill unto itself and some people suck at it then you ll have a harder time than average if your expectations are more reasonable and you re more flexible on location and you don t suck at applying then you ll have an easier time than average', 'instead of school vs no school try and look at it like this it takes x amount of work to break into the job market x can either be spent in school or self learning and adding to your portfolio having a degree is closer to the easy or no questions asked path as in employers know how to handle and evaluate somebody that has a cs degree without it you ll probably have to prove yourself more to employers you would have to have more in your portfolio when compared to somebody with a degree you might look at your school or choice and see what they have besides a cs degree some places have a software engineering path that would focus more on programming fundamentals and less on math science you could also look at their it cis degrees personally that s what i have and my school offered a version that was heavily focused on programming some schools even have a web development option you might see if there is a technical school around you that offers a two year programming degree or cert let s assume that you come out the other side with roughly the same knowledge regardless if you go self taught bootcamps and an undergraduate degree where does it matter you ll probably have a higher chance of getting interviews and or job offers with a degree some larger companies won t even look at you this will only matter for your first couple years in the work force after that nobody will care about your education and will focus on your experience okay i said most companies won t care about your degree this is true unless you decide to pivot into something that s not web application development ai machine learning big data etc might require a degree since they all require knowledge outside of coding']

image image open users amp amp amp amp amp downloads test image jpg test image list list np asarray test image now i had an inverted grayscale image with interger pixel values i e whitespace was coded as black with a value of to convert this i did test image list round float pix val for pix val in test image list then i converted this into a array test image array np array test image list reshape at this point my image was formatted seemingly exactly like the images within nielsen s data so then i looked to nielsen s code he doesn t give any explicit way to classify novel images but it seems return a calling net feedforward test image array returns a x array net feedforward test image array array e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e lots more numbers here e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e e this seems odd to me as that feels like the inputs to the output layer of the network nodes each taking an input from the node hidden layer i was expecting a array with one value close to and the others close to then i noticed further down in nielsen s program within his evaluate function def evaluate self test data return the number of test inputs for which the neural network outputs the correct result note that the neural network s output is assumed to be the index of whichever neuron in the final layer has the highest activation test results np argmax self feedforward x y for x y in test data return sum int x y for x y in test results he takes the argmax of feedforward to determine the result of the net but when i call that i get gt gt gt np argmax net feedforward test image array hahaha i have no idea where that is coming from clearly something is wrong here but i m not sure what my issue is anybody got any ideas', 'neural network tutorial', 'it s a very broad and complicated field and kinda hard to exhaustively answer in a single reddit topic you could just check the wikipedia page for some examples of applications but anyhow artificial neural networks are systems for computation loosely inspired by natural neural networks such as the human brain the basic purpose of a neural network is to take a bunch of input data transform that data via layers of hidden nodes and then produce an output the interesting bit is that you can tune the values inside the network to change the output produced by an input in this way it s possible for the network to learn the common elements between different inputs that should produce the same output and thus be able to solve tasks such as is this input a picture of a cat and others neural networks can basically be used for lots of different sort of tasks such as image recognition voice recognition spam detection and the like they ve also seen use in game ais though mostly on research level diagnostics data mining trading system control modelling real world phenomena such as the weather and so forth the simplest neural network is probably the convolutional neural network which is often used for image recognition you can think of it as a graph of input nodes connected to a bunch of intermediate nodes connected to a bunch of output nodes when data flows into the system it first goes to the input nodes then it goes to the intermediate nodes and is multiplied by a weight associated with the connection of input and intermediate node then it goes to the output nodes and is again multiplied by a weight the cool thing is the ways in which the system can be made to learn for example if you know what output you would like for a particular input you can tune the weights as such that the input gives the desired output if you do this for enough many sets of example inputs the system may eventually learn to produce correct results for input for which you do not know the correct output beforehand let s say you feed samples of handwritten letter a to the network and adjust the weights as such that the outputs always indicate the letter as a now there s a chance that when you give it the th handwritten letter and don t yet know if it s a or not the system will recognize it since it s been taught to recognize the a s a simple convolutional neural network could look like this each of the arrows would represent a weight that is used to multiply the data as the data flows through the system the inputs could be pretty much anything but in the case of handwriting recognition they might be pixel color representing fully white representing a fully black pixel there are more complicated network systems such as ones that form cycles like recurrent neural networks but if you want to learn more about neural networks start from convolutional neural networks they are simple enough to model on paper and understanding how a simple convolutional network learns and makes predictions is still fairly doable personally i found this explanation very enlightening using neural nets to recognize handwritten digits it includes examples in python to get to chapter replace chap html with chap html in the url the most challenging part is understanding the backpropagation algorithm which is used to modify the weights so that they would result in the desired output values given the specific input if it doesn t click don t worry too much about it it ll eventually make sense']

Name: 18_engineering_cs_software_computer
Representative_Docs: ['first off i just want to establish that software engineering is a branch of cs just like ai security algorithms and others it s the branch of cs that deals with managing software projects and developing software products at some universities carleton for sure but there are probably others software engineering isn t even a program it s and option you can take with a cs degree anyway now that that s out of the way answer to your question depends what you want to do if you want to move up into product development and management roles software engineering is the way to go want to do algorithms ai computer vision cs is the way to go except neither of those statements are really true because there s so much overlap between the two programs always check the programs yourself this is a wide sweeping statement that may not be true everywhere that it really doesn t matter which you study both degrees will allow you to do any of those things listed above the main difference is that cs will give you more freedom you ll have more choice over what courses you take and se will be a more structured program with more mandatory course your software engineering courses personally i think those se courses are valuable even though it may mean not getting to take some classes that sound really interesting now i m probably a bit biased due to the fact that i m in se but i ve thought about switching to cs many times and i think i m coming at this objectively for me if you can take it software engineering is the better option for a few reasons software the job and with experience but it makes you more valuable to just have it right out of the gate it s a more exclusive program a lot less people are admitted to se programs than cs giving your degree more value employers are going to see a lot of cs degrees se will set you apart from the crowd and tell employers you re coming to the company with the mindset of an engineer project and product focused on top of that not all universities offer se programs adding to the exclusivity basically ses are a rarer breed you can always take those courses you wanted to take but couldn t after you ve finished your degree no one forces you to leave after you ve completed your degree requirements nothing is stopping you from staying an extra semester to take one two three or more courses sure you could do that with the se classes but there are going to be reserved spots in those classes for the students in se and there might be no spots for students in other programs it might take longer than just one semester too it is waaaaaaay easier to switch from se to cs mid program than it is to switch from cs to se there are a limited amount of seats in all programs and engineering programs tend to have smaller limits my class is students science courses usually have much higher limits if any after admission engineering student culture can be cancer at some times but man it can be fun as hell too everyone hates the engineering faculty except for the nurses because we carry a fake sense of superiority but being on the inside is a blast there are usually tons of events workshops clubs and teams marketed to the engineering students there s a sense of camaraderie among the engineering students but i guess this last point is more of an anecdote and should be taken with a grain of salt anyway that s my case for se but cs is an equally good choice either way you ll probably end up applying for the same jobs and very likely doing the same things', 'so heres the breakdown computer science cs this is pretty much purely programming these are jobs such as data analysis cybersecurity web development cloud development etc software engineering se between cs and cpe you will be learning the engineering curriculum but will be working pretty much exclusively in software programming these are jobs like ai cloud computing computer vision solving engineering problems algorithmically etc computer engineering cpe this is between cs se and ee electrical engineering you learn computer architecture amp hardware as well as programming mostly on the lower level these are jobs from firmware embedded software to board design fpga asic electrical engineering ee circuits and electrical hardware little to no software outside of matlab control systems power systems dsp etc i m assuming computer science engineering is computer engineering but i would look at the description on the university website and see if it matches software or computer engineering as described above', 'i think computer science is more well defined in the us this usually means taking cs cs two intro cs courses a discrete math course a computer architecture course a programming languages course typically comparative an algorithms course and then maybe roughly electives some universities may add additional requirements say an operating system course outside of these courses you would take some math courses say calculus stat linear algebra mostly because cs tends to follow an engineering curriculum so basically computer science tries to teach programming some theory e g algorithms engineering is probably heavier in math since ee tends to favor math physics including differential equations etc which is less important in cs i don t think people generally call it computer science Name: 19_phd_research_phds_need phd
Representative_Docs: ['i keep seeing these how do i do ai ml research without a phd posts over and over again the reality is that if you want a serious career in research then you need a phd your career is hindered without one it will be like shooting your self in one foot before starting a marathon without a phd you are already ineligible for a lot of grant funded research jobs i e fellowships post docs when there s an over abundance of phd s being pumped out of academia it s a huge risk for anybody to take a researcher who has no research training which is precisely what a doctoral degree gives even if you do make it somehow and get a research position it s likely that you will always be playing second fiddle to the phd holders and you will be passed on for a lot of the more senior pi type of roles academic types are also very anal about this unfortunately my question is if you are seriously about research why wouldn t you get a phd being in a doctoral program you are paid to do research have a mentor thesis advisor no external pressure from corporate executives and are in a community of like minded folks who can help you and bounce ideas off each other it really is the ideal setting to do research', 'i went to ut austin and just completed my phd in the field of computer architecture is this what you meant by arch my thesis topic was on accelerating parallel graph algorithms in hardware many people believe that a phd is a specialized degree about an esoteric problem this is incorrect a phd is actually a generalist problem solving degree in which your thesis topic is just a random problem that you try to solve in order to train this skill this is why hedge funds love hiring phds in random fields like astrophysics and computational chemistry and teaching them finance the idea is that if they were able to solve a previously unsolved problem in their field solving a similar hard problem in finance should come naturally gt how important is it that the phd candidate has published papers of note before getting an industry role this depends on your role industry generally doesn t care about publications but having good publications certainly helps there are two types of roles that phds generally land the first type is a role that specifically cares about your thesis topic and is hiring you because you re an expert on this topic a good example of this would be the apple siri team hiring a phd who specializes in natural language processing this type of team won t mind if you don t have good publications if you can prove competency in other ways that s what the interview is for but having excellent publications is how you become highly desirable and get into crazy m bidding wars as is common with top ml phds the second type of role only cares about the fact that you have a phd as a credential which means you should be better at problem solving than the average engineer gt how important is the school that they came from this is generally extremely important recruiters and teams generally are unqualified to properly evaluate a phd s publication record so school quality and advisor becomes a proxy many teams that like hiring phds have specific advisors that they trust and predominantly hire students who worked with a particular advisor your advisor s connections usually end up being extremely important for you to land a position that specifically cares about your thesis topic but going to a top school like stanford will open doors regardless of your advisor gt is it likely that someone with such a distinction would be able to fall back into a tradition developer role if they are not competitive enough to get work on a more advanced team yes this is type in my answer above plenty of companies especially the big like seeing the phd even if the role is meant for bachelor s students it s a nice bonus and the company gets to underpay you given your experience phds generally get hired level above bachelor s', 'you only need a phd if you re going to be doing research and publishing otherwise no you do not need a phd to get into ai i ll mention a quote from machine learning phd guide from hours yc backed non profit from oxford gt would i prefer a path within machine learning that doesn t require a phd gt gt if you re going for machine learning research positions in academia or industry you ll need a phd it can also be helpful to have a phd or even further academic experience to set up a startup that develops cutting edge techniques but for most non research positions in industry including at top firms such as google a master s is sufficient for industry jobs that aren t pure research going on to get a phd isn t an advantage and probably isn t worth the time cost gt gt also bear in mind that many data analysis problems that companies and nonprofits have don t require machine learning to solve often good solutions can be built using simple data science methods instead source']

Name: 22_python_learning_tensorflow_learn

not that hard to learn but even if you re beginner and just got into coding this is amazing course take a look so coming back to the point i need to know where can i learn more i am into game development unity c and recently i ve been looking into data analytics so i would love to learn these things in depth using python so is there something that you peeps can recommend i can pay for a course if it s paid and good enough edit udemy course summery become a python programmer able to independently develop complex applications in python improve your skills with bonus practice activities throughout the course use python for building desktop applications with tkinter use python for scientific computing with numpy use python for building interactive web maps with folium use python for computer vision with opencv use python to download data from various data service apis use python for batch geocoding of addresses with geopy use and fully understand object oriented design build real life python applications in a wide range of areas use python to build web applications with flask use python for database applications use python for data analysis and interactive data visualizations with pandas and bokeh use python for scraping data from websites with beautiful soup use python for sending automated emails with smtplib use python to analyze and visualize stock market data use python to schedule programs based on computer events', 'i have a bsme and have been teaching myself python ml for a few years now and have implemented concepts both at work and in personal projects i can t offer any advice on a masters but you can get fairly far in learning ml concepts from online resources i work more in energy than mechanical hvac but i found that having a me job with access to interesting problems and data allowed me to apply concepts as i learned them which is in my opinion the absolute best way to learn programming ml in general there is a lot to learn and it can seem overwhelming but if you apply what you learn daily and push yourself you will get impressive results to start learn python it s an easy to learn language and there are well supported data ml and visualization libraries additionally many only resources courses use python so you ll be able to follow along start getting used to some data libraries such as pandas and numpy next or at the same time andrew ng s intro to machine learning course on coursera is an excellent starting point for ml concepts i would recommend finding a github repo of the assignments in python and do them in python as the course uses a matlab clone that s outdated not really used anywhere outside the course there s also a paid program from the same instructor that i haven t taken but uses python and may be more up to date after just a few weeks of this ml course you should be able to start thinking of some ways to apply what you ve learned are there any optimization problems to solve if you can get some input output data for say sizing a boiler or something compared to reduction in gas usage you can model a function using linear regression and run gradient descent to find the optimal solution neural networks use the basic concepts of linear logistic regression just applied in interesting ways try to apply what you learn as you go and build on what you ve learned get more complex creative with your projects as you go and try to keep applying concepts as you learn them again we have an advantage working in an engineering field as there are always optimization problems to solve and you can apply many of the core ml concepts to solving these problems as you start to move onto more advanced ai topics after the intro to ml course there are a number of paths you can take personally i found the fast ai courses to be incredible though they are not for everyone and i believe the barrier to entry is higher than they advertise it took a few tries to get into but if you struggle through the first course you ll learn a lot about everything from python working on a command line and getting impressive results with different types of neural nets i would highly recommend getting months of proud of and will be applying soon with a portfolio to back me up i ve considered getting a masters as well i still might depending on how the next months of job search goes and consider myself to Name: 24_questions_interview_interviews_asked
Representative_Docs: ['gt i already tried hackerrank and it sucks to be honest the questions aren t clear they ui is crap and sometimes whatever compiler it is that they use for certain languages isn t up to date with the latest stuff you d be fortunate if an actual job interview uses hackerrank sorts of coding tests most will ask you to code on a whiteboard on paper in order to be able to do that confidently though you obviously need to be fluent when using an ide text editor on a computer gt i may get a shot at an interview at a cool place where they do machine learning i really want to get in there and while i don t know what sort of questions they ll ask i know they are going to ask something have you tried checking sites like glassdoor for sample questions even if your targeted place isn t listed has sparse info related roles and companies usually have similar interview questions and processes gt the last time i interviewed i was very well prepared because i did some of those questions as practice i m willing to do whatever it takes to get into that company but i m solving one or two problems at best per day you could try reading a book like the algorithm design manual it spends a lot of time building your core problem solving skills in the context of programming and also presents a plethora of common problems and their solutions', 'i ve interviewed onsite at both companies got rejected at both companies lol for software engineer at js and quantitative software engineer at ts two sigma was for full time and i made it all the way through the process jane street was for internship and i made it to onsite but only did onsites which leads me to believe they cut it short because i performed poorly apparently this is a thing at two sigma not sure about jane street in interviews at jane street and at two sigma i was asked pretty regular algorithms coding questions for all except one interview the one that was different at two sigma i was mainly asked about linear algebra and a bit about writing code to efficiently solve linear algebra i did quite poorly on this interview but other people i talked to mentioned that the guy i interviewed with tended to give tough interviews other than that i actually thought all the other two sigma questions were pretty easy jane street on the other hand i thought did pretty brutal interviews they are also the only company i ve ever interviewed at who has two people interviewing you at a time during onsites in terms of ways to prepare both companies emphasized to me that they did not need you to know anything about finance they would teach that to you if you were hired i would also say that your standard leetcode grind isn t necessarily your best bet i remember being asked about like functional data structures for example at js which isn t exactly something you typically get in leetcode style problems ts though was mostly pretty standard so leetcode type prep was more helpful another piece of advice would be to get some familiarity with machine learning and do some linear algebra review maybe i had a one off fluke but both companies are definitely very involved in those areas so it can t hurt you you kind of seem to imply that you don t go to a mit stanford caltech cmu etc this is probably going to make it harder for you to get your resume noticed i remember my ts recruiter telling me that they primarily recruited out of like six schools if you do go to one of those kudos i did which probably helped me get noticed learning some ocaml in advance might be a way to do that not required but might make you stand out i didn t know any ocaml i also heard that for the qswe at ts many most people have advanced degrees i was working on my ms so that may help you get noticed but a friend of mine still working on his bachelors also interviewed there different role i think though so who knows i should also note that this is for swe qswe roles at js ts respectively if you re interested in quant trader roles that s a whole different ball game your interview questions are going to be much more heavily math focused if that s the case quants traders are also generally making more money than swe i d also like to push back a bit against they idea that your going to be on a whole nother level at js ts my experience and perception at both was that they are very interesting and prestigious companies with a lot of smart people doing some pretty cool work however these are ultimately fairly small companies a few hundred at js i think about at ts focused on a fairly narrow field there are plenty of people in these companies that do keep the lights on type work and probably fewer who are doing some kind of cutting edge research the research that is being done is likely mostly in areas that are pretty specific to their businesses and you are kidding yourself if you think there isn t boring work being done at these companies that s actually one of the reasons i went for qswe rather than swe at ts qswe is more often involved in building the trading systems and implementing the trading algorithms while many swes work on things that are largely the same as what they d be working on at any other company', 'some of the questions i ve got from ml ds interviews have included probability questions questions about how to clean a specific dataset what does it mean if a model is doing xyz weird behavior usually an overfitting or underfitting question questions about regularization explaining which model to use for a certain situation and why if can be solved with supervised learning i usually just go with randomforest lol my background also has a fair bit of deep learning in it so i ve been asked how does a lstm work and when is it used as well as general nlp questions what is a recurrent neural network etc it depends on your specialization background more than the swe interview does in my experience swe interviews have asked me questions about data structures algorithms and big o time complexity along with the occasional system design question algo questions are usually like delete all duplicates in an array in one pass or implement a queue using two stacks and then describe its complexity they ve pretty much been leetcode questions in my experience sometimes the questions have been very easy and sometimes they ve been pretty tough during my first coding interview not with a big i got a really tough question it s called word break ii on leetcode and it s considered a hard level problem and i was so nervous going into it to begin with that i couldn t calm down enough to get the right answer it really freaked me out and had me thinking that the rest of my coding interviews would go that way but so far i ve been asked comparatively easier questions during my swe interviews some ml ds interviews will focus more on algorithms and data structures though so it s pretty important to stay on top of those']

Name: 25_ai_intelligence_artificial_artificial intelligence
Representative_Docs: ['there isn t a syllabus posted for ai but i m pretty sure it s like an introduction to ai in general maybe it s an elective class from syllabus for the computer performance class computer performance analysis is required in system design system selection procurement and system use tuning main techniques include analytic modeling queueing theory operational analysis simulation the use of simulators and general purpose simulation systems and languages measurement benchmarking based on natural and synthetic workloads by the end of the course the students will be able to identify performance problems in the operation of computer systems utilize measurement tools to identify and eliminate bottlenecks monitor load distribution perform load balancing and improve performance be able to participate in computer capacity planning projects be prepared to participate in computer benchmarking and selection projects write discrete event simulators use basic analytic performance analysis models to predict computer performance and participate in system sizing projects edit artificial intelligence course description gt overview of algorithms and approaches central to artificial intelligence study of fundamental concepts needed to attain human level intelligence in computer systems and gain experience in working with these concepts through assignments and programming exercises topics include problem solving methods heuristic search game playing agent architectures machine learning and various topics selected from knowledge representation symbolic reasoning computational models of virtual humans neural networks and genetic algorithms learn how to research assess and review advances in the field and to consider the application of artificial intelligence techniques to real world problems', 'gt why is there no website not necessarily interactive that teaches people ai concepts there are plenty of them if you are starting out i would suggest looking here afterwards you can look into deep learning frameworks and they have plenty of books and resources online and or look into existing projects or compete with others given a specific subject on kaggle gt i assume it s just a too wide and not yet solid field and things change rapidly that s not true as far as i know elements of machine learning are taught at every half decent university during your statistics data mining courses it doesn t go into an extent of deep learning and cnns but you will likely encounter linear regression heck this is even taught on physics logistic regression decision trees finding anomalies in the data set probability theory etc neural networks from which you see these ai concepts like self driving cars are a very old concept vital concept in training them backpropagation dates back to like it is true that there s a lot of research currently going on in the field because we have reached easy accessibility of huge datasets and computing power to actually analyze them but you would be surprised how many problems we have solved with machine learning without you even noticing first example spam filters these are undeniably powered by machine learning not a hard one mind you you could write a working sample in a day here s an example basically you take email samples and categorize them as spam and ham then you take each individual word in first email let s say it s contents are i am a nigerian prince and have a million dollars and we categorized it as spam let s take words longer than characters cuz others will likely occur in every kind of email and we end up with nigerian prince have million dollars we can say that it occurs in of spam emails we take another email which is in ham category this one says hello son how are your studies going have you helped your sister with schoolwork this time we end up with words hello your studies going have sister with schoolwork words that are new hello your studies going sister with schoolwork we associate them with ham there is a word have that we have seen previously in spam category however hence now we have samples of it one with a value spam one with ham this means it has strength effectively putting as in the middle on what to do with such an email repeat it for few dozen emails and you will see that you end up with a map of words with individual strength eg prince will end up with value making it almost certain it s a spam then when you encounter a new email you take all words in it that occured in your map add them together and divide by their count boom you end up with a classifier that will tell you whether it s more likely to be ham or spam sure it won t be perfect in human languages context matters a lot you would want to look at groups of words and not just individual ones but it s a fully working example of machine learning used for classification we actually use a similar system at my workplace to assign a proper Name: 26_linear_algebra_linear algebra_math
Representative_Docs: ['yes if you learn and become familiar with linear algebra you will be in a position as a programmer to excel in gpu programming graphics simd programming vectorization optimization use and many more in my experience i use in order of utility discrete math linear algebra graph theory calculus pde probability stats knowing linear algebra gives me an edge in my programming field it s a tossup as to graph theory but linear algebra really goes hand in hand with it if you are interested in anything that needs graphics machine learning high performance or parallel computing gpu computing get a good handle on it', 'okay then you are actually super close linear algebra is the big one make extra sure to go above and beyond in that subject even if it means protracted independent studying after the course has ended it s worth it trust me if you were an engineering or physics student instead of cs which i guess you are you likely would have already been introduced to vectors and their basic operations in practical terms vectors are the start of linear algebra but linear algebra is really about the study of linearity which is more or less the property by which hard math problems become solvable at all gt finally we make some remarks on why linear systems are so important the answer is simple because we can solve them so most of the time we solve linear problems second and most important it turns out that the fundamental laws of physics are often linear gt feynman linearity only requires two things of a system f x y f x f y f ax af x if you have these two properties then there is a set of extremely well understood extremely powerful tools you can use to solve your problem exactly approximately analytically numerically however you want so for this document linear algebra covers sections amp let me know if you need any more pointers there such as if you want to follow up with some self study i have some recommendations classical probability theory at least at this level is really just a soup of straight forward calculus with the understanding that probabilities should add up to actually there is not really true they should integrate to this is where that simple but annoying calculus soup comes into play it looks like if you understand why probability density functions can be gt e g why a is false then you re good to go for section you could probably meet this requirement in a day of self study starting from where you are now section is about a very specific very important special function the gaussian they have all sorts of properties that make life easier if you have to do calculus on a probability distribution you probably want it to be a normalized gaussian distribution i don t think you need to memorize all the properties of the gaussian but i do think it is good to be exposed to some of them the first few chapters of prince s computer vision book is one of my favorite treatments of this but if i remember it might be a little inaccessible to you since it assumes you re pretty solid at the multi dimensional stuff let me know if you need a better resource by the way the gaussian looks like n x mu sigma sigma sqrt pi exp x mu sigma often you will consider mu and maybe sigma which can simplify things a good bit that equation might look scary but there are a number of tricks that make it pleasant to work with in practice section is the only thing from vector calculus aka multi variable calculus but it is a big one the gradient and what its good for the gradient is the higher dimensional analog of the derivative and you can use it to find minima and maxima of a function you can do this by hand or my preferred method numerically with a computer never underestimate how much money you can make by being good at numerical optimization once you re solid on the fundamentals of the classes you ve already taken vector calculus and you know a bit more than the fundamentals of linear algebra then you re good to go for machine learning at least by the definition of that study guide but i think that document is actually a very level headed and reasonable assessment of the math needed', 'gt pretty much all sorts of regressions can be understood with some basic linear algebra without doing rigorous proofs of convergence or existence of whatever is behind it many clustering algorithms aren t super tricky things like pca neural nets svm random forests are mostly linear algebra with some functions added on you generally don t need to be able to prove representation or convergence theorems to use in the real world so one would probably want to have proper undergraduate linear algebra probability and statistics under their belt but you don t need to be pulling in graduate level mathematics unless you re researching new algorithms this also ignores the massive engineering portions of practical data science like gathering cleaning data and using the models in real world software much of the math used in day to day machine learning is mathematical statics real analysis linear algebra graph theory and finally multivariable analysis functional analysis an undergrad having knowledge in all these subjects can even begin to real ml ai papers and begin to implement the theory for whatever they are working on']

Name: 28_ai_general ai_true_computers
Representative_Docs: ['my point was mainly that people mainly due to shitty reporting from the media really have a too optimistic idea of when we will have a general ai many people see the reports on self driving cards and computers beating board games as if we re almost there in fact we are barely closer to true ai so the creative broad domain problem solving ai primates or heck even birds than we were a few decades ago the focus has mostly been on technology that has commercial applications like self driving cars automation like that has an enormous market not on fundamental research and stuff like alphago zero getting so much more media attention than true fundamental research doesn t help', 'a general ai won t exist for at least another years there was nothing general about the go ai and in fact ai research has hardly progressed in that direction the main thing that improved over the last years is computing power in the super computer deep blue won from kasparov the first time an ai won a chess match that was deemed too hard for computers nowadays your pc can beat kasparov or any chess master for that matter go is no difference it s a lot more complex than chess but also there it s just a matter of time a true ai is something completely different computers are getting a lot faster making more and more stuff feasible but a true thinking creative ai is a long way away also when software can write software we have a pretty big problem on our hands', 'i m not saying there s no huge improvements in that regard but you re mistaken if you think that alphago zero is in any way applicable to real world problems the approach they took only works on very narrow problem domains gt there have been enormous leaps towards a more general ai that s the point they are really overstating the actual applicability in real life these types of ai s can solve one type of problem really well and as long as you can break down a problem in more of these really narrow problems you can do stuff like have self driving cars but a true general ai does not work that way at all so in fact the alphago zero team have been really overselling the applicability of their work in general ai s does their research help when for example creating better self driving cars definitely does it bring us closer to true general ai barely']

Name: 32_automation_jobs_automated_ai
Representative_Docs: ['yep i ve been reading a lot lately about the original luddites especially in the context of automation and how we ve finally noticed thanks to killer ai that taking the human out of the loop might be a bad thing to be fair two centuries of taking any human contribution out of labour and production has some upsides there are genuinely shitty jobs humans no longer have to do and we all have nice cheap things but we haven t yet replaced it with any other sense of human fulfillment we ve just turned a whole lot of jobs into mechanical labour done mindlessly by humans so now of course they are ripe for replacement which gets really scary when you look at automation in health care and the extent to which it isn t aiding humans who have a genuine vocation so that they can more easily look after other humans and is becoming get rid of the humans so all our sick people are efficiently cared for by cold unfeeling robot arms and the humans who were caring for them can contribute more to the economy amp x b human use of technology took a pretty wrong turn years ago and has never recovered it s not the fault of technology but there s certainly something broken and g internet technology is a classic case of the double edged sword it can make communication easier and bring people together or it can tether people to their shitty jobs after hours and increase the extent to which we are pressured to never unplug and to constantly be online regardless of the quality of interaction', 'gt ai is taking over a lot of jobs will it take over cs jobs as well ai right now is taking over a lot of manual and repetitive jobs which are unfortunately mostly blue collar labor hence the concern that ai is accelerating inequality white collar jobs still have quite a way to go before it loses a lot of jobs to advances in technology right now it s automating certain manual tasks in white collar professions we see that even in programming jobs with a lot of dev environment setups being automated for instance like with website generators but that doesn t mean the entire profession is going away it just makes life easier for us programmers', 'how is it not the whole point of the commercialization of artificial intelligence machine learning and more generally speaking automation is to eliminate jobs contrary to the popular public opinion it s not the entry level burger flippers that will go away first most of the ai we ve been developing and has been successfully commercialized over the past or so years has targeted the medical field with advancements in natural language processing we re seeing developments into ai lawyers and accountants pretty much any job that deals with a wide birth of information that needs to be recalled by the professional is a target for ai in the two or so years i spent working as a data scientist the majority of the hype was about burning the candles at both ends accenture ibm intel etc are all targeting both high paying white collar jobs like lawyers and pharmacists as well as entry level employment there s actually way more money in eliminating high paying white collar work you could argue that automation could potentially produce new kinds of jobs but for a fact we know it is going to shrink the size of the labor market as we know it today the purveyors of automation are passing the buck on to government and moreover a society which has always moved slowly so if this latest generation is to be adopted in a similar fashion to personal computers or smartphones we d losing jobs at the rate of a market crash automation isn t necessarily bad but the pace at which it s adopted is everything']

Name: 33_ai_definition_intelligent_brain
Representative_Docs: ['i agree but not in the way you mean the brain is able to perform sophisticated learning with few exposures and generalize extremely well our process of learning may be able to be modeled as a complex sequence of events well timed electrical pulses release of neurotransmitters deposition of neuromieumators this in addition to evolutuinarily honed architectures give rise to plastic structures how they change has been refined through the eons and very much depends on the most advantageous behavioral output for survival machine learning is data science its pattern recognition i agree the brain does pattern recognition but it also incorporates other motives when utilizing processed information and acting on it ai in my case isn t just how to integrate information and recognize it as a known entity or novelty but also how to incorporate this information into decision making what is pretty amazing about biological networks is that they can quickly incorporate novelty into decision making and that s where plasticity comes into play the ability and to what degree an algorithm can generalize depends on the distribution of data put into the system i still very much love machine learning and did my ms in this area the utility of machine learning grows with all the work being done but for my phd i went back to the brain there are things that data science algorthims optimization and stastics can t capture in the ways we currently use them in machine learning', 'gt also a lot of modern neural nets are a complete black box a human can t understand why the algorithm works the way it does that itself i would argue already counts as automation i absolutely wouldn t machine learning is not automation automl just does some optimizations on the nn and ml algorithms', 'gt here i just gotta disagree even though i totally agree on neural network human brain reinforced learning is totally a thing in human psychology the more times we learn something the better we are at recognizing and identifying it i didn t say reinforced learning isn t a thing for us though just that the approach our brain takes and the approach neural networks take is very different we learn by teaching ourselves to look for distinct stuff for example toads and frogs have certain differences the more distinct the difference the higher the weight so elephants are easy the have a trunk which pretty much no other animal has the difference between a croc or an alligator is harder neural networks don t work that way the don t really look at very specific differences they re trained to look at general equality instead']

Name: 36_computer vision_vision_opencv_image
Representative_Docs: ['my final year project was automatic number plate recognition using svm neural networks i was able to learn apply the concepts of image processing machine learning c opencv svm neural networks and makefile during this project i took reference from this github repo to understand how production ready code for this project can be written this github repo has a free license there is a book called mastering opencv which explains the theory needed to solve this problem you can download the pdf from the internet or purchase it this book github repo can also help you to solve the following problems ch cartoonifier and skin changer for android ch marker based augmented reality on iphone or ipad ch marker less augmented reality ch exploring structure from motion using opencv ch number plate recognition using svm and neural networks ch non rigid face tracking ch d head pose estimation using aam and posit ch face recognition using eigenfaces or fisherfaces ch developing fluid wall using the microsoft kinect', 'i am not trying to discourage you but the scope of your project is beyond massive just today intel got a small portion of what you want working simply displaying stuff on a glasses lens and some simple data processing link they don t even include a camera in their prototype intel is a billion dollar company computer vision imaging is one of the hardest things in programming we use pick the image of a stop sign captchas because programs can t figure it out themselves the best current programs are neural nets which are trained by those millions of captchas if you have a white background and a black shape in the middle it is not trivial to determine if that shape is a square triangle etc and that is the most ideal scenario seriously open paint and make pictures for a square circle triangle then try to make a program that correctly selects the right one every time it will probably take you a few weeks at least throw in some almost but not perfect shapes and see if you can identify those delete a pixel or two in one of the shape walls those very minor things will be next to impossible to account for now imagine your image is moving and you want to identify multiple objects in a scene and there are a few million colors various shades objects are not all one color objects are different sizes based on distance and you want to figure out friction and other forces rolling objects are not the easiest thing to calculate throw in angles and potentially hitting balls off a wall do you know how elastic the table wall is or into each other there are somewhat infinite points you can strike a ball how do you go through them all to figure out which ones give the desired result uh oh one of the balls is chipped or has a piece of dirt on it can your program detect and deal with that now we need to actually display the results figure out more math for overlaying the solution on the image taking account distance perspective and the constant shaking the average human can t be perfectly still do all that in real time with minimal processing power to get it on a phone or tiny chip on a headset it sounds like i am nitpicking some hard stuff but i have not even scratched the surface of problems you will encounter', 'hi guys amp x b i am working on an extra credit project where i am taking a course on computer vision the project is going to be written in python and i hoping you guys can help me clear up a way i can approach this problem amp x b project identify the color of the car and change it for example a red honda civic in a picture change that red paint color into a blue color amp x b now i don t know if my approach is the best but i have been looking into how to approach this problem as i go through the course in school amp x b approach amp x b design a cnn that will detect and classify the image of the car within the picture amp x b design a separate cnn classifier that will detect and classify the color of the car and connect this with the cnn amp x b connect the two cnn together creating a neural network where one cnn will do patch wise detection of the color while the second cnn classifies the image and the car within amp x b create a gan like network that takes the color of the car if the color of the car is already blue nothing happens but if the color of the car is red then the gan will change the color of the car to blue amp x b if you guys see problems with my approach or can recommend looking at different ways to approach this problem i am open to it amp x b btw please do not give me the answer but do point me in the right direction as i want to figure out this problem on my own as much as i can so that i can be a better programmer amp x b i am going to be updating my approach regularly the more i learn about computer vision and the responses i get from this post i will also be posting up code to so that other people can understand how the problem is approached']
