Name: 40_teachers_teacher_kids_education
Representative_Docs: ['teachers are venting on the i want to speak to the manager parents not all parents and people do it here where certain sense of anonymity is guaranteed because they are professionals and they don t want to do it in front of the students or spread the stress to someone near them gt gt i hate to break it to you all but of americans feel they are underpaid furthermore with student debt in the trillions a lot of us are having a hard time justifying why we should dish out any more money to an education system that is failing in preparing us for the problems of tomorrow don t put that shit on us if anything teachers usually are the ones who would support candidates who are vouching for free affordable education if you are pissed off about those conditions maybe focus the folks who are continuously voting for tax cuts and shortening funds on education and various other publicly funded social safety net social infrastructure and while i agree on how current education system is not serving our young kids well it is bit outdated model that is still stuck between industrial era to service era when we are moving beyond the service era with further development in robots ai please don t think that development of youth is teachers sole responsibility social capital and various other factors at home can contribute to their development and those are something that cannot be controlled by the teachers', 'well i have no special expertise on this topic but i have plenty of opinions this is reddit after all to me the really fascinating thing is that no one is asking your question we just jumped right to how can we use data analysis to get all of our kids to score above average which is the wrong question for about ten different reasons for one thing it assumes that data analysis all by itself will improve learning we already know how to do it schools already have all the data they need since they re already measuring all the right things and the best use of achievement data is determining teacher pay and even firing a few people but here s the thing if this is so effective why aren t we doing it with the kids i mean when we want to measure student performance we measure student performance when we want to measure teacher performance we also measure student performance what this tells me is that the people who love data analysis don t know what good teaching looks like if they did they d measure that so here s a question we should probably already have asked is present year student achievement a reliable proxy for present year teaching quality really the reason we measure teacher performance through paper and pencil student achievement tests isn t because that s the best way to do it it s because that s the easiest way to do it it s cheap doesn t take a lot of time and gives an easy to understand number also since we were already doing it it wasn t too far out of our way but suppose for a moment that we really were measuring everything we needed to measure how would you evaluate a teacher who produced better test results than her peers by say a full standard deviation but lets add some wrinkles her students are also more likely to drop out of school and they exhibit more mental health problems in future years is that a good trade off what about the teacher who keeps the troubled kids in school and away from drug abuse and legal problems but doesn t produce great test results what about the teacher who raises test scores among high achieving kids but whose low achieving kids are worse off or the reverse well the good news is that we aren t measuring any of those things so we don t have to make any complicated decisions and i think that s kind of the point all of these initiatives to improve education through data seem designed to make life easier for administrators and politicians at the expense of teachers and students so can data driven decision making work in schools yep i think it can but we have to ask the right questions first and then figure out what data we need right now we re looking at what data we already have and designing our questions around that but you could use machine learning to predict who is likely to drop out of school not in a national sample but among your own flesh and blood students what does that high school dropout look like when he s in the th grade there are clustering algorithms that can tell you whether there are sub types of dropouts that might need different kinds of interventions which kids are failing classes in high school despite having average or better academic skills what do they have in common what about those kids who have a primary caregiver who is not a biological parent what does their educational career look like you probably have all of this information sitting in databases in your school district right now and none of it is being used to help kids or improve education well this turned into a manifesto sorry about that but to me the bottom line is what it s always been we d rather not do real education because it s messy and complicated and hard we d rather just stick to training since it s cleaner easier to measure and easier to report to the public meanwhile school continues to be the place where kids go to learn to hate learning', 'the role of education technology is continuously expanding from projectors to smart boards that enable increased interaction between teacher and students in the classroom we are saying various aspects of education technology becoming an inherent part of the educational experience for students teachers parents and management alike there are of course casualties the primary one of these is the elimination of now obsolete job roles that have been taken over by a piece of technology for instance the work of an accounts department is largely automated by your school management software this platform allows for the generation of fee structures that are tailored to individual students raise dues and generate invoices taking into account various conditions that might be imposed on a student by student basis using a school management software reduces the amount of human effort to be put in in addition to reducing the human rate of error but does this extend to teachers with the advent of machine learning natural language processing and robots that are lifelike in a way never before seen how far away are we from an automated teacher let us begin by taking a step back what is the role of a teacher it is not merely to teach but to provide an education there is an art to imparting knowledge to students that current technology is nowhere close to mastering educational technologies are currently aiding teachers in their work not completely taking over them they are tools not replacements we can use school management software to collect data on student learning patterns check for the efficacy of a new learning method or just make the teaching experience more holistic and continual by using technology to facilitate engagement these certainly enhance the teaching experience and the learning experience for students however they are not comprehensive enough to completely take over the task teachers do more than just the one way task of instructing a student they identify social cues that would be impossible for a machine to parse especially non verbal or invisible interactions that affect the learning experience they help identify roadblocks for students that might be more personal or emotional in nature that a machine cannot pick up on they help to contextualise lessons in real time which might not be possible for a piece of technology to do think back to your favourite teacher did you enjoy her teaching because of her infallible library of knowledge or because of how she made the subject she taught come alive for you the human connection is necessary for something as key as the act of learning recap what is the future of school management software apps in institutions instead of asking whether technology has reached a point where it can replace human teachers we must ask what aspects of the job it can take over to make their lives easier teachers world over are overburdened they take on a caring role in a parent s stead they advocate for students who might be otherwise forgotten and they shape a nation s future early on instead of merely remembering them on a designated day teacher s day cards are appreciable but not game changing we should perhaps be focusing on building technology that aids them in these efforts school management systems already perform these roles in different ways they help teachers open up communication channels with students and parents teachers are able to stay better organised and on top of their game with reduced time and effort investment thanks to the superior organisational and processing capabilities of their school management software education technologies can help enhance teaching methods by making existing content more dynamic or helping teachers create fresher content that was previously out of their reach due to budgetary and technological constraints technology must be built to enable teachers not replace them']

each year in the us x more people graduate law school and pass the bar gt k than people get a mere bachelors in physics lt k similarly more than twice as many people pass medical school each year gt k than get your same undergraduate degree just a few years ago the ratios were twice as high too i ve got the same degree did a couple years of grad school too so i know you just feel exhausted to use a graduates is so much lower for a reason this should replenish a bit of your confidence one of the other responders is suggesting bootcamps i think they fundamentally misunderstand the type of learning and training that those places provide is not intellectually satisfying especially not to the type of person who would choose physics over the alternatives i think bootcamps have a place but most people also just don t understand what type of value a person with a physicist s background can bring to the table in software you do not have a competitive advantage in being a web dev but this does not mean you don t have a competitive advantage your position and story is sounding very familiar it reminds me a lot of myself i graduated with my bsc physics when i was i m also a jack of all trades one of the reasons i wanted to pursue graduate study in the first place was to reach the point where i could study independently successfully by myself to get to the point where i could take on large projects without supervision this is what the phd is supposed to signal to the rest of the community right well at a certain point i realized i did not need to burden myself with the rest of the bullshit of academia to meet this goal plus i really wanted to make a lot of money while remaining intellectually engaged so i started training myself to fulfill a niche in my case this meant i became a computer vision engineer and developed skills in modern c performance optimization gpu acceleration computer architecture numerical optimization software engineering best practices bayesian stuff such as probabilistic robotics and function approximation a less fancy word for machine learning i also learned as much domain specific knowledge as i could by reading textbooks and doing literature reviews especially on the state of the art on the slam and sfm tasks simultaneous localization and mapping and structure from motion i focused on maximizing the robustness of the vision system instead of improving any individual metric a lot of those skills were things that i had taught myself already and i just managed to find a domain where i could leverage a large number of those skills i drilled down into the problem until i identified a tiny almost insignificant niche where i could make a contribution i provided the fastest feature descriptor and descriptor matching implementations available it was a bad paper by research standards i did nothing new though i did identify a promising avenue of research but it was a very practical body of work it solved a real problem better than other people i won t lie though it was a thoroughly mediocre paper but i flew to amsterdam and presented it at a conference it was work no one told me to do that my advisors didn t even understand write a fluid sim on shadertoy or learn cuda or do something that moves you forward to your actual goals computer graphics and computer vision seem like natural ways forward for you i really think you advance for the long post tldr at the bottom tho hi reddit so i am currently a mechanical engineering major transferring from community college to university this coming fall and i am at a crossroads i am right now trying to decide if i want to stay in mechanical engineering or switch to computer science so let me give you some brief background but if you don t care for it and just care about my opinions on both majors then skip the next two paragraphs so i didn t originally declare as a mechanical engineering major because i had any passion for engineering or math or physics for that matter i chose it because i was somewhat decent at math and i thought i could challenge myself and get a degree that will get me a good paying job with a good work life balance even though i did not love what i was studying it didn t matter because there really weren t any fields out there i was passionate about they say do what you love but all i love to do is enjoy myself by going out and seeing movies and spending time with close friends and family playing video games and reading books working out etc etc basically i feel the same sort of interest for m e as i would for any other engineering or any other subject for that matter so i was hoping maybe some of you guys can help me decide help me see more clearly so i am basically picking my future career path for a few reasons work life balance money i know some of you guys don t like to hear it when somebody picks there major just for money but for me it s a very big factor industry options i e i don t have to be stuck working in one industry like say chemical engineers mainly working in petroleum here are my views on mechanical engineering i enjoy the math and problem solving creating tangible things from scratch sounds so appealing to me but i feel for the most part that isn t what mechanical engineers do especially when they work in big companies it seems the pay for mechanical engineer s peaks at much lower rates than those who work in software it also seems that most or at least a lot of mechanical engineers end up leaving engineering anyway usually into management or other more business type positions after a decade or so i am aware also that a m e can always learn how to program and career change to something software related while the same cannot be said for computer science majors vice versa but is it in my class were enthusiastic about them but i felt like i had to drag my miserable self through them to get them done so basically i am one of those guys who thinks the idea of working with tangible things and building things is awesome but in actuality it may just not be for me i will say that of all the physics i ve taken mechanics always clicked with me the most so here are my views on cs again i actually really enjoy math and i think cs and math go hand in hand while engineering especially m e is more geared towards physics i have taken two programming classes python and java my professors for both sucked so i found myself not loving programming but certainly not hating it in fact there were times where i enjoyed the puzzle solving nature of what i was doing the pay right out of undergrad is much higher than m e up and coming fields like a i machine learning and data science all seem appealing to me i ll be honest sitting in a cubicle all day coding is not ideal but once again it doesn t really matter to me in the grand scheme of things as long as i can get a good work life balance and make a decent wage computer science projects beats physics labs any day of the week for me i m also concerned that the market for computer science will soon become over saturated since there is a lot of hype regarding the major and perhaps it will soon be much harder to find work and the pay will decrease so if you read through all that i thank you i know it may seem from reading it that i already made up my mind and want to switch to cs but maybe some of you guys can still convince me that engineering is the way to go or maybe some of you guys can give me a reality check on cs not really looking to double major or minor or anything like that i want to decide between the two once again thank you again for reading tl dr been studying mechanical engineering for past few years transferring from community college to university now and having second thoughts thinking about switching to computer science not sure what to do and hoping for some advice']

Name: 44_systems_computing_theory_cs
Representative_Docs: ['there are many things computer science is a broad field that covers all science that has to do with computers programming languages and programming in general is a small part of this field as you mention yourself algorithms and data structures are a large part of computer science not only knowing what they are and how they are used but the ability to design new ones for specific problems and understanding and proving their complexity w r t runtime memory requirements etc machine intelligence and machine learning are also good examples of popular areas within computer science right now other disciplines ui design specifically focused on the psychological theory behind choice of colours placement and grouping of elements and more systems design specifically focused on providing a development team with a coherent and manageable structure for a complex system that is to be built without systems design completing a large project is practically impossible database theory specifically focused on the mathematical definitions and properties of relations and database systems by understanding the math that database systems are built upon you will understand how to design a database that contains a minimum amount of redundant data has no data loss when joining tables query optimisation and more computer architecture operating systems and the low level aspects of software allowing you to reason about what is actually happening when your program is running why certain errors happen why batch loading data into main memory with buffers is a good idea how when and why parallelism and concurrency works etc mathematical modeling and verification of software systems etc there are many extremely interesting fields try to orient yourself superficially around the field of computer science and dive into something that catches your eye', 'page of the catalog completion of at least thirty credits at the graduate level level and above with no grade less than a c in any course and a minimum gpa of at least twenty one credits must be from computer science courses identified by the cs prefix at least three of the courses must be drawn from this set cs principles of programming languages cs concurrent programming cs data mining and knowledge management cs introduction to operating systems cs tcp ip networking cs mobile systems and applications cs introduction to cloud computing cs enterprise and cloud computing cs interactive computer graphics cs artificial intelligence cs human computer interaction cs web programming cs enterprise software architecture and design cs distributed and cloud computing cs database management systems i cs fundamentals of cybersecurity cs privacy in a networked world cs advanced algorithm design and implementation cs parallel programming for many core processors cs advanced computational modeling in biology and biomaterials the remaining nine credits can be from computer science or any other disciplines some students may seek to take electives that form a focused area of study accordingly several suggested focus areas of logically related electives are defined including cloud computing databases gaming and simulation mobile systems modeling simulation and visualization network and systems administration secure systems software development visual computing and information extraction web application development see the computer science department web site for definition of these focus areas and the course descriptions start here', 'to expand on this cs is a ridiculously broad field in general it s answering the question what can we do with a computer as a person living in the st century i m sure you realize the answer to that is ludicrously broad and all encompassing as it s matured the field of cs has invaded and joined with many other disciplines here are some interesting areas and what they look at computer security a lot of cross over with number theory pure math and stats tries to answer the question how can computers communicate securely machine learning basically just statistics with some optimization and some ideas from statistical mechanics this area is by itself insanely broad people attended nips one of the largest ml conferences this year while some people may be pure ml researchers in general they will have a particular interest in some other field and use ml as a tool for solving problem in that area natural language processing machine translation lots of statistics ml here but also some cognitive science which tries to model how humans use language this also encompasses speech recognition although those researchers may be more in the ece department computer vision lots of linear algebra and ml images are d representations of d space computer vision tries to answer the question from these images what can we recover about the d space computational bio lots of stat ml bio too tries to model biological systems also includes computational genomics human computer interaction psychology ml tries to study how humans and computer robots interact this field uses a lot of psychology for understanding the human side and then a lot of ml for modeling those behaviors in robots computation theory algorithmic theory pure math what in theory can a computer do how do we model a computer so that we can prove what it can do how long does it take to solve these problems what is the fastest a computer could solve this problem expect a lot of proofs here and not a lot of programming some of the things people do here would probably never be used in practice there are also lots of sub fields in here big data pure math statistics so the previous field did cool stuff but as they showed none of their algorithms will ever finish when you have petabytes of data how can we get approximate solutions can we use probabilistic algorithms and bound the probability of getting the wrong answer databases you have a shit ton of data how do you efficiently store it and access it computer networks how do we get lots of computers to efficiently and securely communicate this is somewhat related to parallel computing which is asking what sort of algorithms do we need to get millions of computers to work together on the same task there are soooooo many things you can do in cs alternatively pick another field add computational in front of it and you ll have a subfield of cs or at least something you can do using your cs education math is used everywhere some subfields are more focused on statistics and modeling while others are more into pure math proofs and number theory my advice is to get the intro classes up through data structures out of the way and start taking at upper level cs class per semester to broaden you re exposure find a professor who seems interesting take their class and do research in their lab you ll learn so much and get a feel for whether you actually like their sub field learn python every school does their own thing in terms of what is used in classes but if you re interested in more mathy fields you ll probably want to know python it s very popular in scientific computing']

Name: 55_article_salary_grads_soccer
Representative_Docs: ['gt there was even an article recently talking about how ai graduates were able to make more than a quarterback joining the nfl did you actually read the article the ones profiled and mentioned in that article weren t just any phd s they worked either directly with heavy hitter names or were prominent researchers already the article mentions ian goodfellow whose book deep learning is widely read and popular it also mentions ilya sutskever another prominent name who worked under hinton at toronto and was a post doc at andrew ng s ml lab these aren t your average joe s at any state university it s like saying soccer players are making more than million and then profiling some big names in european soccer doesn t mean a soccer player on your local sports club s sunday league team is gonna rake in that money', 'i worked at google for several years in a senior role fair warning about anecdotes like this they re not totally off base but there are often several underlying misunderstandings that distort the truth google hires virtually all lower level t t candidates as generalists regardless of background there s no such thing as an ml ai position though there are teams that do that sort of work and candidates with relevant skills and credentials are more likely to be matched with them after the hiring process if a particular team really wants a candidate there are levers that managers or recruiters can pull in an attempt to push out a stronger offer but these still need to be approved by the compensation committee and generally can t go out of band so it s not automatic there s a special hiring track for phd graduates that will place them directly into a t mid level swe role straight out of school but the bar for that is particularly high for obvious reasons a lot of phd candidates are ultimately just hired at the same level as a normal new grad t k is technically achievable even as a t it would be fairly low for a t especially if you have strong competing offers and or a few recent years of industry experience most ms grads coming in that high have one or both of those i saw below that this particular individual has a couple years of work experience and completed the ms while working so there s your answer it s not because they have an ms finally i ve noticed a tendency for people especially new grads to inflate the real value of their offers by front loading things like one time bonuses and relocation packages a lot of k offers are really more like k when you appropriately amortize these over the likely life of the job', 'i just read this bloomberg article that says google facebook apple amazon com uber technologies and others dangle dazzling pay packages to lure top academics to work on teams developing facial recognition digital assistants and self driving cars even newly minted ph d s in machine learning and data science can make more than beyond the tech industry among those betting on similar expertise tailored to their interests are banks hedge funds carmakers and drug companies this makes it sound like every new grad is making k straight out of school and if you re not then you re a failure is this really the case i can imagine superstars like andrej karpathy making that much straight out of school but what about the average phd student with or average papers does anyone have a more accurate sense of what of new grads really make that much is it the top and just a few dozen exceptional superstars or is it the top and at least several hundred or thousand new grads']

Name: 57_bootcamp_bootcamps_cs_boot
Representative_Docs: ['gt and more importantly they definitely don t teach you years of cs material in months true but they are not trying to because most of it is a waste of time unless you want to head into something like machine learning that requires a very specific skillset a good bootcamp grad can hold their own against a cs grad with ease in the real world cs courses teach you theory but a good bootcamp will teach you how to be a developer that s not to say that a cs degree is worthless i d love to go back and do one at some point but if all op is aiming to do is work as a full stack developer finding a good bootcamp is a seriously valid option that said there are a lot of crappy bootcamps floating around and people just blunder into them without doing any research the course i attended had an entrance test and a very tight acceptance rate they guarantee a job at the end and count some of the uk s best known companies and most respected companies amongst their hiring partners that s the type of bootcamp you want to look for amp x b', 'i would say if you want to do the bootcamp route i have friends who did that then yes do it however i would like to suggest that especially for web dev you really do not need to go through a bootcamp or get a cs degree i have self taught myself everything i needed to know about the mean and lamp stack by myself through things like stackoverflow teamtreehouse and the like and was able to land a paying internship in my opinion as someone who still thinks over these three routes all the time i have come to the conclusion that cs is for people who should go to it if they intend to work on something low level like c c stuff or intend to make contributions to the realm of cs such as ai machine learning ect bootcamps are for people who want to get a foot into tech in the most efficient and fastest way possible if you do intend to go this route i would highly suggest research the bootcamp because i know from friends i have talked to that certain bootcamps like coding house are not that good and sketchy at best in what they teach and the curriculum in general do not go to bootcamps that are teaching stuff like ruby and ruby on rails as they are extremely dated and really make sure that if you go to bootcamps you know what you are getting yourself into i assume you only have done like or java classes and maybe an assembly class and then decided that web dev is much more fun and easy to do as someone who is now a junior in his cs degree i can tell you there is much more to cs than just programming in java c there is a rich depth of theory that i feel like a lot of people tend to not appreciate at the undergraduate level and i feel like the first couple courses of cs don t do the field justice they are more or less just there to filter out people that do not belong in the major in the first place with all that being said the self taught route is also very good and with a good degree of conscientiousness and discpline you can very well achieve the same level of competency going the self taught route as you would the bootcamp route with around more or less the same time you can even just look up the curriculum some bootcamps use and just follow them by yourself using online resources whenever you re stuck there is in reality very little to gain from going to a bootcamp vs self teaching for web dev speficially the only thing s you miss out on are networking and ceritifcations people vouching for you which is ultimately something for you to decide if its worth the extra k cost to get at the end but speaking personally tech demands more so how well you know your shit way more than the connections you have established more than any other field i worked at i remember having to do a pretty hardcore whiteboard interview back when i was applying for my paid internship for example i honestly think the best route for you would be to go into web dev with the self taught route and then just going into the job market and landing a job i also feel like you will eventually burn yourself out because of how awful javascript is to work with especially nodejs and will want to come back to finish your cs degree and do something else more low level hope i helped op', 'disclaimer i have intimate experience with bootcamp grads and there is a big difference between doing cs and going to a bootcamp most bootcamps will give you skilled labor familiarity i e you learn how to do that one particular job you will most likely not go on to be the leading ai researcher or building the next os if you catch my drift a cs major opens up many more possibilities allowing you to choose your specialization in software my advice would be if you have the time and money get a cs degree it will open many more doors but if not a bootcamp can be a viable option my experience with bootcamps i used to teach at a coding bootcamp as a result i know many people who have graduated from them there absolutely is a stigma around them and it is an uphill battle to start a career you will need to go into it expecting that you will face many rejections and many companies ignoring you but starting a career as a software engineer is possible i ve seen bootcampers who are very successful and have transitioned into roles at bign amp unicorns there are even some that land roles at bign companies right out of bootcamp but these are usually people who have stem degrees first off my experience was in san francisco and location does matter there are startups on every corner out here and tons of opportunities secondly not all bootcamps are created equal do your research and talk to alumni before spending any money the majority of the bootcamp grads usually end up at a small ish startup for their first jobs they work for less than market rate pay and build up experience eventually with enough learning and networking they get more high profile lucrative jobs and move to better positions at larger more desirable companies there are also many bootcamp grads who just can t make it either the job hunt is too harsh or they lose motivation they just end up wasting the money they spent on bootcamp and go back to doing what they were before many bootcampers are career changers trying to get to a place of comfortable living with a decent career and a bootcamp can do that for someone i d be happy to give you more info in a pm if you re interested gl e commas']

Name: 59_ml_machine_machine learning_learning
Representative_Docs: ['tl dr i want to know if an interest in the algorithms and mathematics behind ml will carry over into an enjoyment of the day to day work of an ml data scientist analyst amp x b i m early in my career i have about years of experience doing mostly web dev i m still tying to figure out what i like some restructuring is happening at my company and my manager asked me what areas i m most interested in working on the company is starting to use machine learning for predictive analysis so that s an option that s open to me my only experience with ml of any kind is an online udacity course i took on deep learning neural nets i found it really interesting because i love algorithms and math my degree was in physics very math heavy my concern however is that the daily tasks will have little to do with algorithms and have more to do with pruning data i don t really know what that entails but it sounds kind of boring maybe i m wrong so my question to those in the field is what does your daily work look like does it involve an understanding of the algorithms does it scratch that itch so to speak', 'i recently joined capital one as a deep learning machine learning researcher in the nlp domain for optimizing their service chatbot eno my typical day is pretty similar to tip r i run models on public and private internal datasets tune hyperparameters to optimize perplexity scores and use python tensorflow for tweaking model architecture i think the best part of a i industry research is that you get to work on some really tough problems with a lot of cutting edge technology for example our team gets to use several maxed out nvidia gpu cores to train our models you also get to publish papers if you re primarily on the research side of things travel to conferences present results and even patent and get paid for them technologies that you ve devised during your work the flip side to all of that is that you ll most definitely need at least a masters and phd for research roles to get into this field you might get lucky and find industry positions network your way within a company into one of these roles w o those credentials but that d be the exception not the norm i d highly recommend andrew ng s deep learning machine learning courses on coursera if you re interested in learning more about the field great content and also a gentle introduction to a i machine learning', 'i worked at a ai startup managing aws infrastructure and gpu servers i scouted the field and the roles for this are research software development engineer rsde this role is for masters or applied phds you will work with crud and the latest unstable libraries to integrate models from pure research into prototypes or ship into production at this point most of the cool ai stuff is packaged in shit coding standards and your job is to just clean it up and make it into an api data engineer basically you are cleaning data but depending on how good you are at automation and crud design your focus is on the quality of life of the data management of your research org not a lot of research work but people will blame you if the data is not perfect some statistics masters end up here because they like aggregating data machine learning engineer once someone has cleaned the data how do you use keras aws ml api or etc to do proof of concepts this is mostly for small business or when your org outsources you to consult no hardcore research but most businesses have no need for the latest algos a simple regression model is your goto a lot of the times no degrees necessary so long as you know how to use an api and collect data researcher you studied a lot and know a lot about a narrow section of ai you stay up long hours cleaning data to squeeze more accuracy on your models you truly believe that your work will bear fruits in years as of now all the models only have limited applications to you problems as i realized i did not have the aptitude for real research and did not enjoy how chaotic research code can be i decided against doing a masters in this field and ultimately quit this job my researcher coworkers said that you should only pursue a master s if you really enjoy crushing datasets on kaggle regularly for fun or if you find yourself building computer vision projects at home on the weekends if you have that passion great then the hardships of dealing with egos in academia will be worth it otherwise you are going to feel miserable and not see that much more of a bump in salary at the end of it']

control systems and am debating between two courses course robotic vision and applications to understand basic robotic vision theory used in the advanced manufacturing automation relate robotic vision theory to the robot kinematics students will learn how to identify the components of a vision system install vision hardware develop an application program the robot perform error recovery procedures and follow recommended safety practices it involves both classroom instruction and hands on training labs assignments and projects will be done using industrial robots fanuc s iw fanuc lr mate ic and fanuc cr ia collaborative robot simulation and off line programming will be done using visual components and roboguide simulation software packages in this course which covers industrial robotic theory and applications students have opportunity to receive fanuc d irvision certificate if they successfully complete the required test course deep learning fundamentals amp applications introduction to the theory architecture and application of artificial neural systems supervised unsupervised and reinforcement learning in a single and multiple layer neural networks self organizing maps learning capacity and generalization gradient based learning methods and deep learning networks application to image recognition pattern classification nonlinear modeling and forecasting i m currently registered for the deep learning course since it was posted first the deep learning course is an ece department course while the robotics course is engtech but counts towards my degree which course do you recommend to help with getting into control systems in industry i ve herd that deep learning is still relatively new for use in control systems to replace pid controllers thanks', 'so i actually did a double major in undergrad and have a ba in computer science to go with my bs in engineering which was in mech engr but heavy on robotics i also have two years industry experience time off between undergrad and grad but that was a software dev job my point here is that i m very familiar with robotics concepts and have taken classes in artificial intelligence machine learning robot motion planning computer vision at the undergrad level as for markov decision processes i ve studied those in a planning and learning robotics ece course at the grad level i ve also taken a bunch of optimization rigorously while i do like and enjoy all of these practical robotics material i have a slightly stronger affinity for the mathematical rigor and purity of doing a controls phd and my ideal career would be where i can do some complex theoretical control design of the time and do some applied stuff of the time also funny that you mentioned ford i m actually starting a month internship at the ford research and advanced engineering center in january next year where i ll be working on some delay systems research that will hopefully culminate in a paper i ll be using a cpt for that since i m an international student but yeah post graduation i have the software dev option microsoft amazon et al because of my cs degree and year software dev industry experience but i ll rather have the control robotics distribution i described earlier so places like tesla uber et al would be great my only problem is that software places pay more and considering all the credit card debts i m accruing money would be a critical factor when i m job hunting anyways thanks for your response and sorry this ended up being more like a resume listing than a direct response to your comment lol', 'i m still a student in computer engineering and don t have much industry perspective but wanting to go into robotics as well i plan to go to grad school to pursue robotics have some prior published research on robotics and nasa internship building them was part of a first robotics team in hs robotics is broad and multidisciplinary ranging from mobile robots like the vex bots robotic arms drones swarms robotics in automation assembly lines lots of research opportunities and practical applications the best choice for you is going to depend on what you re interested in are you interested in how to design them me how to control them at low level circuits controllers ee how to control them at high level ml ai vision etc cs you will benefit greatly from having some overlapping knowledge in all areas but for a career working on complicated systems you ll want to specialize in what you re interested in i d rec looking at the degree plans for your uni read the little course descriptions find out which ones sound interesting google the ones you may be concerned about and get an idea of why it s taught etc you don t need experience in robotics to succeed as an entering student your first priority should be your core math and physics as they will help you develop the intuition necessary to dabble in different areas additionally i d work on a personal project if you re interested in mobile robots for ex i d pick out some motors h bridges and a raspberry pi then design a little d printed chassis if uni has a makerspace print it there cheaply or buy the parts to assemble one and just explore putting together your own little robot from scratch with the raspberry pi you can learn to write some code to make the robot move i recommend python and from there you can add do just about anything you think of with it to answer your last q i graduated hs with an associates degree completed and could have finished my cpe degree earlier for me personally i gained a lot in some of my buffer classes and the time as an undergrad has given me an opportunity to explore what i m interested in while i have the time to do so i definitely like to take my time though and don t feel pressured to run out and get a job an internship is very important and i would not trade it for an earlier graduation date it may not be possible to do it all in yrs if your classes have lots of prereqs have to take class a before b then b before c despite how many hrs you have credits for i think ee with cs minor is an excellent choice']

Name: 66_vision_computer vision_geometric_computer
Representative_Docs: ['okay which parts what side of big data ml because honestly the sad truth is that a lot of the work in ml is very boring the core algorithms are neat but the infrastructure around it is not as enjoyable that s my take yours may differ how much math do you know read szeliski s free textbook it is a nearly complete overview of the entire field as of when it was published major omissions are slam and deep learning decide if you want to go into geometric computer vision or learning based computer vision you can probably get a job in geometric computer vision once you can sling together a demo project that shows visual odometry i did at least vo is the workhorse of structure from motion and slam aka understanding shape from pictures i have a repo around here somewhere which has a minimal near pedagogical implementation of vo there are four key steps to understand keypoint detection descriptor extraction descriptor matching with ratio test or similar ransac to resolve epipolar geometry this is a little more tricky but i have some resources that can simplify it once you get to it focus on getting good correspondences first to get up to speed on the state of the art first read the orb slam paper and papers it cites and the ones they cite etc until you understand it this should take a while it is a literature review just doing this more or less makes you employable at the msc level on the learning side there are a bunch of things that i am familiar with but not an expert on learn about random forests and neural nets also try the books probabilistic robotics and prince s models learning amp inference ask me once you have a more clear picture what you want to learn', 'i m an open book a masters degree can be really good it is the most common path into this field actually more of my coworkers have phds than msc i have a bsc but i m a little weird the researchers have phds and the engineers have msc or phd georgia tech is probably the best school in us for this type of thing most of the schools focused on geometric robotic style computer vision are in europe switzerland germany and france especially but not exclusively the computer vision programs in the us are mostly focusing around deep learning in terms of how you can start building your skillset make a software rasterizer amp or maybe a ray tracer or photon mapper make a visual odometry demo there are four steps to vo feature detection descriptor extraction descriptor matching and epipolar geometry estimation wrapped by ransac read szeliski s book survey text does not cover slam or deep learning read probabilistic robotics you need to master linear algebra and numerical linear algebra likely want to learn about lie algebras and geometric algebras as well read tom drummond s notes on lie algebras and the book on geometric algebra by alan macdonald learn numerical methods especially numerical optimization such as levenberg marquardt preconditioned conjugate gradient automatic differentiation and the schur compliment trick learn how to write very fast clean modern c including simd and assembly all the normal software engineering stuff you need for any cs job learn how orb slam works read the original paper be prepared to do a pretty onerous literature review by really digging into those citations and their citations learn at least the basics of machine learning amp deep learning if you have a degree know most all the stuff above and have work experience you won t have much of a problem breaking a third of a million dollars of income a year in the bay area i m a pretty financially motivated person but once you re making gt k yr post tax income probably isn t the part of your life that you need to focus on optimizing you can always go into finance or entrepreneurship if you want to i have a friend who is literally one of exactly ten people in the world with her skillset none of them are ever unemployed and she has opportunities thrown at her there are a lot of companies she can offer absolutely nothing to what does it matter who is going to come and take her job who is going to come and take mine when so many people so loudly say that you don t need to learn math for cs if you want to be a web developer go be a web developer if you want to do computer vision do computer vision you re accredited have experience know math and how to code you don t need to cast your net any wider find what you want and specialize in it also in one case we took someone as an intern who had done a postdoc given that you can imagine we weren t really looking for any undergrad interns so when you think about it having a hard time finding interesting opportunities before you specialized is very unsurprising the skills in computer vision are transferable i have a standing offer to go to naughty dog to work on graphics for the last of us for actual fps in engine gameplay on a console click this link i moonlight for nasa working on the parker solar probe at my day job i make magic glasses the skills all overlap the bedrock is numerics and the ability to self teach', 'gaining experience is the slowest part of all this gaining maturity as a swe is front and center on your critical path computer vision is a great great field you get to use math who would have thought lie groups would be useful outside of university work close to the hardware be rewarded for writing fast code many cv people lack this so it can be a defining competitive advantage but might not block you either use machine learning in anger not from an ivory tower google brain has to actually go and find companies with cv problems to help if you re on the other side the problems come to you and that s much more satisfying for me personally be on the cutting edge of research solve problems that ve literally never been solved before and deploy them to huge scales geometric intuition and spatial reasoning skills are really very useful and its fun you get to make pretty visualizations a thing that is irrationally close to my heart and it pays crazy well i also consider the field sexy but that is very nebulous the skills are transferable the barriers to entry are large there are exploitable niches all over the place and the demand is sky high and that ain t changing any time soon it s a damn good field to build a career in computer vision is kind of a field divorced from itself let me give a taxonomy within cv there is geometric cv and machine learning cv within learning cv there is deep learning and classical methods there is a significant amount of fad chasing going on with deep learning it is an amazing tool but beware of being swept up in the hype the entire field is throwing baby out with bathwater right now and over emphasizing deep learning to the point of madness we had a cv intern from a good university with a msc in computer vision who didn t know what a camera projection matrix was not forgot the form of it but literally had never heard of it to solve this read all parts and give special attention to the decomposition at the bottom of part however i much prefer to think about the projection operator as being separate from the intrinsic or extrinsic matrices much of this is because of how academia operates divorced from reality most papers do not talk about compute cost or even consider practical applications of their work random forests or better yet hybrid methods continue to dominate if you look at bits of information gain per flop yet you ll find nary a soul who is aware of this because no one in academia has hardware well enough constrained to ask questions like what is the best full computer vision system i can make given this computational resource budget they re all just gaming metrics in isolation which when removed from isolation usually don t mean as much as you d think this fervor is all exploitable of course be mindful of which way the current is flowing but do not hesitate to swim the other way i ve found that doubling down at the things i find interesting and the people in my field are now neglecting always been bad at has blown many doors wide open it doesn t necessarily help me get into the most mainstream desirable of places but there is so much good work demanding to be done on the fringe that i m not really crying about it and to be clear it doesn t rule out big n at all computational perception books if you re really going to do this thing get the following books there are free legal pdfs of the first few and they re where you should focus your attention for now so maybe grab physical copies once you have income szeliski survey text outdated but useful omits slam and deep learning numerical methods for computer vision machine learning and graphics what it says on the tin learn everything in this book even if you don t learn it from that book i d also supplement it by making sure you re comfortable using levenberg marquardt with a robust loss function which is a glaring omission prince s models learning amp inference bayesian methods good pedagogy and beautiful graphics invitation to d vision heralded as a more accessible hartley amp zissermann which is a universally used text on geometry you should also buy probabilistic robotics some specific applications outdated but the ideas arent clear derivation of kalman filter amp co criminski amp shotton s random forest book very simple approach to ml with rf requiring very little math while providing the big ideas i don t agree with all the perspectives but its a strong starting point supplement with paper global refinement for random forests goodfellow just because dl is a fad so huge it is destabilizing the entire cv community doesn t mean you shouldn t use it when its the right tool for the job i ve not read this one but i hear it so consistently mentioned i m going to put it on this anyways bayesian methods for hackers much lower priority unique book niche but fun methods using probabilistic programming there are more books worth reading but i think that is a very solid reasonably well rounded core there is also an endless sea of papers to supplement each book of course right now i d skim through szeliski and quick skim the numerical methods book to just get a lay of the land first few chapters of prince deserve a read and serve as a standalone introduction to applied probability amp statistics other nice gems are hidden throughout such as a clear presentation of the so called schur compliment trick which is widely used in optimization schemes other things are lower priority unless they really call to you except as called out below but i d maybe try to get a handle on the ekf from probabilistic robotics i think they actually cover it in two separate chapters regardless the core takeaway from that text is that the whole damn distribution matters not just the mean or mle or map and under reasonable assumptions layering uncertainty on uncertainty always increases your certainty projects for projects i suggest tinyrenderer make a forward mode automatic differentiation class using operator overloading absolutely underrated technique do not waste your time fucking around with implementing analytic jacobians until you ve frozen your algorithm and are optimizing performance implement an inner product using sse yes literally something the simple but do it simd congratulations you now know simd which is shockingly rare once tinyrenderer is done start on these make a naive visual odometry pipeline but do it as much from scratch as you can that means keypoint detection use classic fast keypoints feature extraction use an upright latch descriptor copying the pre learned triplet locations feature matching all pairs brute force rejecting points which do not have satisfy second nearest neighbor distance nearest neighbor distance lt k and or nnd snnd lt ratio epipolar geometry estimation wrapped by ransac which means assume a calibrated camera draw a random subset of k correspondences generate a random transform between cameras using algorithm of rosten et al s paper improved ransac performance using simple iterative minimal set solvers a bit non standard but far better and easier also gives you something interesting to talk about note follow up paper by lui amp drummond too tally all n k points as either being in agreement with model or outliers repeat until time runs out or you find a really good model return model with fewest outliers use tinyrenderer to provide input with known ground truth compare result of ransac with ground truth figure out some way to visualize all this i recommend dear imgui but maybe sfml sdl opengl ros or even threejs obviously you should do the vision stuff in c but visualization in threejs is a damn nice party trick implement extra trees extremely randomized random forests in c once that s up link against liblinear to provide global refinement open source it open source everything you do that you are legally allowed to this task is actually pretty accessible to a newbie and would be a pretty nice contribution to the community as no such public implementation exists despite it smashing benchmarks just not the same benchmarks dl smashes i m prevented from releasing my implementation c because i Name: 71_ml_ml ai_ai_spam
Representative_Docs: ['i am a fullstack software developer intern and i mainly do frontend development i am also interested in ml and ai although i never delved deeply into these concepts i know the idea and how some algorithms work i know how to use some libraries to build some models however i don t really get how these technologies are used in software development in the industry are these actually used in the software development process is it totally separate from software dev if i am interested in using these technologies when developing apps what shall i focus on any kinds of replies would be great thanks', 'there are many applications of machine learning also note that ai machine learning to be technical also ai itself is an ambiguous term some are simpler algorithms you can implement manually over few hours and yet are super popular and used in everyday software some are top of the line based on newest theories that we have just started working on if you want some places you can start at look at naive bayes or k nearest neighbours knn these are taught at universities during statistics data mining course simple but their capabilities are fairly good for instance most spam catching algorithms rely on naive bayes the way it works is very simple take an email it consists of words obviously now if an email is a spam then you give each word a score for now that email with these words is a spam then you take more mails and rate them as spam or ham count your words you will find out that for instance word million occurs of the time in spam category you just created an algorithm that now can categorize new incoming emails based on data you have indirectly taught it indirectly as you just clicked on whether an email is spam or not you didn t weigh words yourself manually knn on the other hand is useful for showing connections between users used on netflix like platforms to give movies recommendations aka you use it to find people with tastes similar to yourself what genres they have seen what are their average scores and so on if they like a given movie you haven t seen it means you will like it too bigger catch than actually using a machine learning algorithm is preparing data for it in the case of the above naive bayes you can get a lot of worthless noise for instance since almost every mail includes you or i you also need to ensure that theyre they are they re will be kept as same record in your database otherwise classification process will take longer and be less accurate than it could be same with knn for instance if you have people with similar tastes but drastically different ranking score then they should be close to each other aka both adam and steve love horrors but adam never gives a score higher than and his average is like steve on the other hand will give to any title he enjoyed so you would need to normalize your data taking average score into account this is the same with more complex machine learning algorithms you see those crazy uses of self driving cars automated comics coloring face detection etc but it s made with multiple simple layers feeding each other starting from one that will detect basic shapes on an image then going to one that can look for details on these shapes etc input data goes through multiple filters before it finally arrives to the point when you can get your solution does this work like eli for you', 'i am in the aero field and i am about to start a month project working on applying ai methods to my area fluid dynamics from my perspective i d say that over the next years the methods developed over the last years in fields like ai machine learning and data science will be increasingly applied to the more traditional engineering fields i ll use my project as an example convolutional neural networks cnns are an ai method developed primarily for image recognition at its simplest an image recognition cnn is mapping from matrices red green and blue pixel values to a large vector of output probabilities e g each output is the probability that the image is a picture of a certain thing at a higher level this is just mapping between spatially related input data i e nearby pixels are more related than far away pixels you can t mix the pixels up or the data changes and a large list vector matrix of output values in engineering we often have spatially related input data we want to analyse be it a sound or electrical signal a pressure or velocity field in a fluid a stress field in a structure recently work has been done showing that applying cnns to fluid dynamics problems can yield faster results than in the way we would classically do it divide the space into small grids solve the governing equations at each space at each time step and then move forwards in some situations i will be working on a project to see if we can do this with other fluid methods in other situations this is a joint project between where i am fluids research and an ai company we don t have the ai skills and experience but the ai company doesn t have the fluids knowledge to work on this area so we are working together i think this is a good example of how things will change in engineering lots of new methods have come out of computing maths data science in the last years but they haven t been applied yet to existing engineering problems there will be lots of opportunities to improve our current methods with new techniques i think especially in signal processing which is very data heavy there will be lots of ways to potentially apply new techniques i was keen to learn more about ai so i took this project i think a good way to get more up to speed with ai etc it is to try and find something similar applying methods from ai machine learning etc to your field you need both people from the ai background and the existing field elec eng in your case if you can become familiar with the new methods then you ll be in a good position for the future edit changed a few words for clarity']

Name: 72_algorithms_problem_graphs_data structures
Representative_Docs: ['gt i m also wondering what algorithms entails exactly as i m currently at the beginning of semester one algorithms is a broad term but what it generally means is ability to find an efficient solution to a problem or being able to tell if a problem has no quick solutions eg consider a binary search algorithm for instance i give you a dictionary and want you to find a word troll you could just start from page look at every single word till you encounter a troll will take you a while but it s doable but you could also start from middle of a dictionary let s say at l and say well t is supposed to be further than l so you ignore a to l completely try something in the middle between l and z so you end up with s you know t is supposed to be a litttttle further than that so you flip a page and boom you have a t and your troll this is also how binary search algorithm works this way you get an ability to find a word in a dictionary assuming it had a length of words in attempts on average rather than if you just started from first page and flipped them until you encounter what you need it s an important skill for cs graduates to have for multiple reasons firstly it comes up during job interviews a lot if you couldn t code something like binary search or quick sort then your chances of getting hired are that much lower google for instance can give you questions like this one you get decent understanding of trade offs in computer science so you can make a decision on whether you should use a hashmap linked list tries some kind of a binary tree an array etc on whether or not it s worth having your data sorted on how to find a shortest path from a to b you will see that sometimes there s no easy way of finding an optimal solution eg travelling salesman problem they teach you problem solving so how to split a bigger problem into smaller sub problems how to express a real life situation in code and so on meaning it s a must have gt is this something i should really try to do on my own if you get a decent book and there are hundreds of them then you can grasp basics in few days doing it properly aka implementing your own data structures and ways of manipulating them from scratch takes much longer than that and is generally a separate subject at university but it s still doable solo i do suggest to do it after you have basics of programming down however so you can think in terms of a problem and not of language syntax it s definitely something any programmer should know not necessarily at an expert level as you probably won t be working with machine learning and ai which are also algorithms albeit a bit more complex but it s important to have a solid understanding of all basic building blocks as you encounter them everywhere sometimes even unknowingly', 'not sure if this is a simple response but here s a few points first of all basically anything you do in programming is an algorithm an algorithm is just the description of a computable process things baked into a language are usually expressed so simply that no one really calls these things algorithms they are just things you can do while loops sorting filtering etc sometimes you care about the nuanced details of these especially in ex sorting and having a guarantee lists will always be sorted the same even for plain business projects maybe most of the time you don t really care second of all there are popular algorithms people should at least be aware exist binary search and binary trees for example because like they have been proven to be the most optimal ways to do things at scale where you don t just have a flat list of cached memory addresses there s also stuff that s used heavily when people need to implement their own languages or underlying tools that power languages or platforms hashtables stacks and queues for example i think a lot of other things are just to showcase the type of problem solving people can do to that end although it s not popular interview philosophy i don t think algorithms are super useful to know in isolation it s cool if you study algorithms while also learning how to implement your own programming language or your own database or your own data caching service something like that there s a class of problems that can be solved through algorithmic thinking that you may encounter in your career if you want to get into any new and developing field for example there s usually some crafting of your own algorithms because solutions to problems haven t all been prepackaged into architecture patterns frameworks and libraries yet ai iot machine learning is a place that s really algorithm math intensive most people in the ai machine learning space need to know algorithms well enough to be able to tweak them especially if they want to build anything cutting edge lastly and perhaps most importantly knowing algorithms gives you an appreciation for computational complexity how long do things really take to compute i m not expecting my co workers to invent new algorithms every day or something like that that d be a waste of our client s money i do need my co workers to understand that some of their sql queries or javascript callback functions take wayyyyyyyy more time than they should to process and that the app is going slowly because they have registered the callback to recompute an entire dataset every time a button is clicked rather than taking advantage of caching or hashtables for quick lookups i mean i had to argue not too long ago with my fellow senior engineers that javascript objects and arrays don t iterate through entire lists like for loops that they use virtual tables or hashtables under the hood used to be hashtables now more optimal v tables while i was trying to convey that i didn t want us using for loops unnecessarily everywhere across our code and that if we had an index to pass it in and use it i ve always had an interest in computer science so this kind of thing was a no brainer to me i don t necessarily know how to implement a hashtable or v table but i could at least appreciate how it works and that we should be taking advantage of it', 'gt what are the really specific ways that people use data structures in industry whether its for web mobile embedded firmware or any other field data structures like lists hashmaps and sets are ubiquitous in code you can see this for yourself by looking through the source code of random open source projects those fundamental data structures are used everywhere for all sorts of things knowing about common adts like stacks and queues are also useful because it s useful to have vocabulary to help clarify your thoughts for example suppose i m writing an image manipulation program similar to photoshop or ms paint i could say something clunky like let s implement undo redo by adding operations to a list where we can only add and remove from one end but it s much easier to say something like let s implement undo redo by adding operations to a stack graphs and trees are both very flexible data structures you can model all sorts of things using either data structures for example take graphs here are just some examples of how people have used graphs off the top of my head model the political affiliations of bloggers make each blog a node each link to another blog an edge run similarity metrics and see if any clusters emerge graphs are an intrinsic part of google s pagerank algorithm you model the internet as a graph and use that information to deduce what the most important pages are based just on the link structure more precisely you essentially take the graph treat it as a sparse matrix then compute the first principal eigenvector of that matrix model the overworld of videogames for example take games like civilization how do you implement pathfinding for ai and player units git a version control system uses graphs extensively specifically directed acyclic graphs dags each commit is basically a node each node points to the parent minor aside some people try and learn to use git by just memorizing the relevant commands and what they do this doesn t usually end up becoming an effective way of learning git since git s user interface is pretty clunky the easier way of mastering git is to realize that git is just a somewhat clunky way of manipulating dags in arbitrary ways so if you can visualize your repo history as a dag and think of what operation you want to perform you can invariably find a sequence of git commands to do just that with only a little bit of googling suppose you re writing linters and other tools for statically analyzing code you can represent your code itself as a graph control flow graphs import graphs etc if you re a biologist you might be interested in modeling the way genes and proteins influence each other inside cells it turns out you can model exactly that using graphs suppose you re writing a database how exactly do you internally model sql queries well as it turns out you can model them using trees which are really a kind of graph suppose you re trying to solve some np complete problem for example let s say you re trying to find a good solution to the traveling salesmen problem for some logistics startup it s extremely unlikely you ll be able to find an efficient solution in the general case but can you maybe find approximations or good enough solutions see sat solvers gt for what purpose in industry are certain data structures and algorithms used and how i think you re approaching this from sort of the wrong perspective data structures and algorithms aren t specific techniques to be used in specific circumstances the purpose of your classes isn t to teach you a grab bag of tricks to use rather what you do in industry is solve problems sometimes novel problems sometimes boring problems and everything in between so how are your cs teachers supposed you prepare you for that clearly it s impossible to expose you to every single type of problem you ll ever potentially encounter and teach you what to do in each case for one it would take over a lifetime to do that and for another that strategy will fail the moment you run into a novel problem so what do they do instead they pick a subset of interesting and foundational problems to consider walk through them and have you implement or analyze them for example here s a very basic problem how do we represent data that maps one value to another can we organize our data such that given a key we can look up the corresponding value efficiently it s a relatively basic problem all things considering but there are many interesting solutions and tradeoffs to consider we could use a balanced tree a hash map maybe even just use a linear search in some cases the hope is that by studying problems like these you ll indirectly acquire experience problem solving with code in general and become acquainted with a set of useful foundational ideas that you can potentially expand on or adapt as you continue to solve more problems in the future after all problems build on one another for example let s take the problem of mapping keys to values this is already a solved problem right just use a hashmap or something well what if we have so much data that it won t fit in ram we need to store the majority of it on disk instead is a hashmap still appropriate if not can we adapt it to be more optimal find a different data structure altogether what if we need to store the data across multiple machines what if we want to find a range of values instead of a single one e g the way a database can look up a range of indices and of course not all problems will require you to apply data structures and algorithms to solve for example suppose i want to make my ui in some webapp as easy as possible for people to use so i can maximize the number of people who decide to sign up and try my startup or something well data structures won t really help us here instead what might help is having some background about the fundamentals of human computer interaction understanding how to conduct user surveys and interviews understanding how to collect and interpret interaction data understanding the fundamentals of design and aesthetics understanding the psychology and wants of your typical customers and that s fine different problems will ask you to tap on different kinds of knowledge the fact that we might not end up really needing to use data structures and algorithms when doing ui work doesn t make that knowledge less useful in general it s just less useful for that specific task after all there s a reason why data structures and algorithms aren t the only thing covered in a cs degree']

Name: 73_solution_problem_automated_ai
Representative_Docs: ['gt it means the company is looking to replace office desk type workers with an automated solution they basically have what they feel are too many people working on a problem that can likely just be automated away and want to either stop having to hire more of those people as their business grows or replace their current workforce with an automated solution so why do you specifically need ai for this isn t of software written to remove repetitive menial work done by humans anyway isn t this why we invented computers in the first place', 'gt ai solution the persons in charge of that listing management hr etc see as if it were some magical super smart entity that if implemented there would put them miles ahead of the competition this type of thing is a red flag suggesting you would be under pressure to deliver impossible results while being subjected to nonsensical demands gt what would you offer to a company that says they want this i would not offer anything at first instead i would asking probing questions about how they use machine learning or ai already what they would like to accomplish what percentage of my job would be working on this ai versus other tasks they re probably lying about how much ai they really do if i like their answers and i think i have something to offer like my math background i will ten mention my math background would probably prove useful gt even if they don t know what they re talking about that doesn t mean they don t have a problem correct i hate the phrasing problem but rather they have a business need it s likely that business needs will have to be addressed with a real world solution gt how do you both fix their problem while satisfying their ai requirement again preferably not fixing a problem but rather addressing a business need if i wanted the job i would probably keep my mouth shut about how i think it s a dumb idea get hired and continue to keep my mouth shut i would also do the best job i could but understand that my orders might be misinformed and stupid but it s what they re paying me to do', 'you re getting a lot of trash answers from people unqualified to answer this question so i ll give you an actual serious answer it means the company is looking to replace office desk type workers with an automated solution they basically have what they feel are too many people working on a problem that can likely just be automated away and want to either stop having to hire more of those people as their business grows or replace their current workforce with an automated solution it s different from machine learning because machine learning usually refers to a class of algorithms that make use of large amounts of training data to tailor itself to find a solution to a problem']
